## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following questions strictly follow **KTU 2024 Scheme** Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) with **internal choice** as per the official ESE template. All sub-parts carry equal marks.

---

### 📝 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Inter-Beat Interval (IBI) and explain its clinical significance.**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):
- **Definition (1 mark)**: IBI is the time interval between two consecutive R-peaks in an ECG, expressed in milliseconds.
- **Mathematical Expression (1 mark)**: $\text{IBI}_k = \frac{n_k - n_{k-1}}{f_s} \times 1000$ ms.
- **Clinical Significance (1 mark)**: IBI is the fundamental input for HRV analysis, used to assess autonomic nervous system balance, cardiac risk stratification, and parasympathetic/sympathetic tone.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between time-domain and frequency-domain HRV analysis. List two parameters from each.**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):
- **Time-Domain (1.5 marks)**: Operates directly on NN intervals in the time axis. Parameters: **SDNN**, **RMSSD**, **pNN50**.
- **Frequency-Domain (1.5 marks)**: Operates on the Power Spectral Density of resampled NN series. Parameters: **LF power (0.04–0.15 Hz)**, **HF power (0.15–0.4 Hz)**, **LF/HF ratio**.

---

### 📝 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q(A). (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** for R-peak detection in ECG. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**(a) Block Diagram (3 marks)**:
The Pan-Tompkins algorithm consists of five sequential stages:
**ECG → Bandpass Filter (5–15 Hz) → Differentiator → Squaring → Moving Window Integrator (N ≈ 150 ms) → Adaptive Thresholding → R-peak Output**.

**(b) Stage-wise Explanation (4 marks)**:

1. **Bandpass Filter (5–15 Hz)**: A cascaded low-pass (cutoff 12 Hz, 2nd-order Butterworth) and high-pass (cutoff 5 Hz) filter isolates QRS energy. It suppresses baseline wander (≤0.5 Hz), 50/60 Hz powerline interference, and high-frequency muscle noise.

2. **Differentiator**: The transfer function $H(z) = \frac{1}{8}(-z^{-2} - 2z^{-1} + 2z^{1} + z^{2})$ emphasizes the steep slope of the R-wave (high $dV/dt$).

3. **Squaring**: $y[n] = x^2[n]$ makes all samples positive and amplifies large QRS amplitudes nonlinearly, suppressing T-waves and P-waves.

4. **Moving Window Integrator**: $y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x^2[n-k]$ where $N \approx 0.15 \cdot f_s$. This produces a smooth envelope capturing QRS energy and suppresses noise.

5. **Adaptive Thresholding**: Two thresholds (signal level and noise level) are updated beat-by-beat using the formula:
$$\text{THR}_{\text{new}} = 0.125 \cdot y_{\text{peak}} + 0.875 \cdot \text{THR}_{\text{old}}$$
A peak is declared as an R-peak when it exceeds both thresholds within a 200 ms refractory window.

---

**(b)** List and define the major **time-domain HRV parameters**. Compute the **mean HR and SDNN** for the following NN series (in ms): $\{850, 860, 845, 855, 865, 850\}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**(i) Time-Domain Parameters (3 marks)**:
- **Mean NN**: Average of all NN intervals.
- **SDNN**: Standard deviation of NN intervals — overall HRV index.
- **RMSSD**: Root mean square of successive differences — short-term vagal index.
- **pNN50**: Percentage of successive differences exceeding 50 ms.
- **NN50**: Count of such differences.

**(ii) Computation (4 marks)**:

**Step 1: Mean NN**.

$$\overline{NN} = \frac{850 + 860 + 845 + 855 + 865 + 850}{6} = \frac{5125}{6} = 854.17 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: Mean HR**.

$$\overline{HR} = \frac{60{,}000}{854.17} = 70.25 \text{ bpm}$$

**Step 3: Compute deviations squared**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(850 - 854.17)^2 &= 17.39 \\
(860 - 854.17)^2 &= 34.03 \\
(845 - 854.17)^2 &= 84.03 \\
(855 - 854.17)^2 &= 0.69 \\
(865 - 854.17)^2 &= 117.39 \\
(850 - 854.17)^2 &= 17.39 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Sum and divide by (N−1) = 5**.

$$\sum = 270.92 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Variance} = \frac{270.92}{5} = 54.18$$

**Step 5: SDNN**.

$$\text{SDNN} = \sqrt{54.18} = 7.36 \text{ ms}$$

> ✅ **Result**: Mean HR ≈ **70 bpm**, SDNN ≈ **7.36 ms** (low value due to short recording window).

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**Q(B). (a)** Explain the **frequency-domain analysis of HRV**. Discuss the physiological significance of LF, HF, and the LF/HF ratio. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**(i) Method (3 marks)**:
The NN interval series is first **uniformly resampled** (typically at 4 Hz using cubic spline interpolation) to obtain an evenly sampled signal. The **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** is then computed using:
- **Welch's Periodogram Method** (most common): Segment into 256-sample windows with 50% overlap, apply Hanning window, compute FFT, and average.
- **Autoregressive (AR) Modeling**: Used for short segments with better spectral resolution.

**(ii) Frequency Bands (2.5 marks)**:

| Band | Range (Hz) | Physiological Correlate |
|------|------------|-------------------------|
| **VLF** | 0.0033 – 0.04 | Thermoregulation, slow humoral mechanisms |
| **LF** | 0.04 – 0.15 | Baroreflex-mediated; Sympathetic + Parasympathetic |
| **HF** | 0.15 – 0.4 | Respiratory Sinus Arrhythmia (RSA); **Parasympathetic** |

**(iii) Clinical Significance (1.5 marks)**:
- **HF power** reflects **vagal (parasympathetic) tone** and is abolished by atropine.
- **LF power** reflects mixed sympathetic and parasympathetic activity, modulated by baroreceptors.
- **LF/HF ratio** indicates **sympathovagal balance**; a high ratio indicates stress/sympathetic dominance, while a low ratio indicates relaxation/parasympathetic dominance.

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Poincaré plot** for HRV analysis. Derive the expressions for **SD1 and SD2**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**(i) Poincaré Plot (3 marks)**:
The Poincaré plot is a scatter plot of $NN_i$ (x-axis) vs. $NN_{i+1}$ (y-axis) — each point represents the relationship between two successive beats.

**Geometric Features**:
- **Line of Identity** ($y = x$): Points lying here represent identical successive NN intervals.
- **SD1 (Short Axis)**: Standard deviation perpendicular to the identity line; measures **short-term, beat-to-beat variability** (correlates with RMSSD).
- **SD2 (Long Axis)**: Standard deviation along the identity line; measures **long-term variability**.

**Shape Interpretation**:
- A **large, elongated "comet"** = high HRV (healthy).
- A **small, round cluster** = low HRV (pathological).
- An **asymmetric or triangular** plot = autonomic dysfunction.

**(ii) Mathematical Derivation of SD1 & SD2 (4 marks)**:

**Step 1**: Define successive differences.

$$\Delta_i = NN_{i+1} - NN_i$$

**Step 2**: Variance of the differences.

$$\text{Var}(\Delta) = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1}\Delta_i^2$$

**Step 3**: The variance of NN itself is $\text{Var}(NN)$.

**Step 4**: By rotation of axes by 45°, the variance along the new axes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SD1}^2 &= \frac{1}{2}\text{Var}(\Delta) \\
\text{SD2}^2 &= 2\,\text{Var}(NN) - \frac{1}{2}\text{Var}(\Delta)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5**: Take square roots.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SD1} &= \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}\text{Var}(\Delta)} = \frac{\text{RMSSD}}{\sqrt{2}} \\
\text{SD2} &= \sqrt{2\,\text{Var}(NN) - \tfrac{1}{2}\text{Var}(\Delta)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Clinical Note**: A reduced **SD1/SD2 ratio** suggests poor vagal modulation, often seen in diabetic autonomic neuropathy.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units** in IBI (must be in **ms**, not samples). Examiners specifically deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 2. **Writing RMSSD without the $\sqrt{}$** — students often forget the square root and present the mean of squared differences.
> 3. **Confusing SDNN with SDSD** — SDNN is the std of NN values; SDSD is the std of successive differences.
> 4. **Missing the physiological interpretation** in frequency-domain questions — examiners **demand** the autonomic correlation (LF → sympathetic, HF → parasympathetic).
> 5. **In Pan-Tompkins, skipping the squaring stage** — a full block diagram **must** include all 5 stages: BPF → Derivator → Square → MWI → Threshold.
> 6. **Using the word "average" instead of "standard deviation"** for SDNN — strict terminology is checked.
> 7. **Not mentioning artifact rejection** (20% rule) in IBI generation — a critical step in the pipeline that examiners expect.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- ⭐ **IBI = (R-peak index difference / $f_s$) × 1000 ms**.
- ⭐ **NN interval = clean IBI** after excluding ectopic beats & artifacts.
- ⭐ **Pan-Tompkins Algorithm Stages**: Bandpass (5–15 Hz) → Differentiator → Square → Moving Average → Adaptive Threshold.
- ⭐ **Time Domain**: SDNN (overall), RMSSD (short-term vagal), pNN50 (vagal).
- ⭐ **Frequency Domain**: HF = 0.15–0.4 Hz = **Parasympathetic (RSA)**.
- ⭐ **Frequency Domain**: LF = 0.04–0.15 Hz = **Baroreflex (Symp + Para)**.
- ⭐ **LF/HF Ratio** = Sympathovagal Balance Indicator.
- ⭐ **Poincaré SD1** = RMSSD / √2 (short axis).
- ⭐ **Poincaré SD2** = $\sqrt{2\,\text{Var}(NN) - \tfrac{1}{2}\text{Var}(\Delta)}$.
- ⭐ **Standard Sampling Rate**: 360 Hz (MIT-BIH), 250 Hz (clinical).
- ⭐ **Recording Duration**: 5 min (short-term) or 24 h (long-term).
- ⭐ **High HRV = Healthy ANS**; **Low HRV = Pathological/Stress**.
- ⭐ **Task Force of ESC (1996)** is the gold-standard reference for HRV standards.
- ⭐ **Artifact Rejection**: Reject beats deviating >20% from local mean IBI.
- ⭐ **Cubic spline interpolation** is the standard method for NN series resampling.
- ⭐ **Welch's Method** with Hanning window is the standard PSD estimation technique.

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spectral Modeling and Analysis of ECG Signals** is the process of decomposing the Electrocardiogram (ECG) time-domain waveform into its constituent frequency components using parametric and non-parametric estimation techniques. The objective is to characterize the **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** of the ECG, which reveals clinically significant information about the autonomic nervous system, myocardial ischemia, ventricular repolarization abnormalities, and arrhythmia substrates that are not directly visible in the time domain.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this experiment is placed under **Module Experiment 2** of PCBRL507, which is a lab-oriented course. The student is expected to record/process a real or simulated ECG signal and subject it to spectral analysis tools such as the **Periodogram**, **Welch's Method**, and **Autoregressive (AR) Parametric Modeling**, then interpret the resulting spectra against known physiological benchmarks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Recipe Decomposition" Metaphor**
>
> Imagine a chef tastes a complex curry and tries to figure out *which spices were used and in what proportion*. The curry is the time-domain ECG signal — a single, mixed waveform. **Spectral analysis is the culinary equivalent of running the curry through a chemical spectrometer**: it tells you exactly how much of each "flavor frequency" is present.
>
> - The **P-wave** in ECG is like the mild cumin (low frequency, low power)
> - The **QRS complex** is the sharp chili hit (mid-high frequency, high power spike)
> - The **T-wave** is the lingering aftertaste (low-mid frequency)
> - **Noise and high-frequency artifacts** (muscle tremor, mains interference) are the unwanted "burnt bits"
>
> Just as a spectrometer tells the chef *“too much chili, not enough coriander”*, a PSD plot tells the doctor *“QRS peak is at 12 Hz, indicating bundle branch block”* or *“High-Frequency QRS components are attenuated, suggesting myocardial scar tissue.”*

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physiological Frequency Bands in ECG Spectrum:**
>
> | Component | Frequency Band | Clinical Significance |
> |-----------|----------------|----------------------|
> | **P-wave spectral energy** | **0.5 Hz – 5 Hz** | Atrial depolarization |
> | **QRS complex energy** | **5 Hz – 40 Hz** (peak around **10–15 Hz**) | Ventricular depolarization |
> | **T-wave spectral energy** | **0.5 Hz – 8 Hz** | Ventricular repolarization |
> | **High-frequency QRS (HF-QRS)** | **40 Hz – 150 Hz** | Myocardial infarction, late potentials |
> | **Mains (Line) interference** | **50 Hz / 60 Hz** (India: **50 Hz**) | Powerline noise to be filtered |
> | **ECG sampling frequency (standard)** | **$f_s \geq 500$ Hz** (typically 500–1000 Hz) | Nyquist criterion for QRS preservation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in the lab:** In your MATLAB/Octave experiment, when you plot the PSD, the dominant lobe should appear in the **5–15 Hz** region. A *shift* or *broadening* of this lobe is a clinical red flag.

## 1.4 Learning Outcomes Aligned to KTU 2024 COs

By the end of this module, the student should be able to:

- **CO1:** Identify the spectral characteristics of standard ECG waves (P, QRS, T).
- **CO2:** Compute the **Power Spectral Density** of a sampled ECG segment using both non-parametric (Periodogram, Welch) and parametric (AR model) methods.
- **CO3:** Select an **appropriate model order** for AR spectral modeling (using AIC / MDL criteria).
- **CO4:** Correlate observed spectral features with **clinical pathologies** (ischemia, arrhythmia, conduction defects).
- **CO5:** Implement the experiment in MATLAB/Octave and produce a properly annotated PSD plot for the lab record.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The "Why" Behind Spectral Analysis

The ECG is a **quasi-periodic, non-stationary** signal: its statistical properties (mean, variance, frequency content) change slowly over time due to respiration, autonomic modulation, and pathological events. To extract diagnostic information robustly, we transform it into the **frequency domain** where periodicities, dominant rhythms, and frequency-dependent abnormalities become visually and quantitatively explicit.

### 2.1.1 Two Philosophical Approaches to Spectral Estimation

> [!NOTE]
> **Approach 1 — Non-Parametric (Classical) Methods**
> Treat the signal as a finite set of samples and apply the **Fourier Transform** directly. No assumption about the underlying data-generating model is made.
> - Examples: **Periodogram**, **Welch's Method**, **Bartlett's Method**, **Blackman-Tukey**
> - **Pros:** Simple, robust, no model-order selection needed.
> - **Cons:** Suffers from *spectral leakage* and *high variance*, poor frequency resolution on short data segments (a major problem in beat-to-beat ECG analysis).

> [!NOTE]
> **Approach 2 — Parametric (Model-Based) Methods**
> Assume the ECG (or its windowed segment) was generated by a known linear system driven by white noise. Estimate the model parameters, then derive the PSD analytically.
> - Examples: **AR (Autoregressive)**, **MA (Moving Average)**, **ARMA**, **ARMAX**
> - **Pros:** High resolution on short segments, smooth spectra, fewer parameters.
> - **Cons:** Risk of model mismatch; requires model order selection (AIC, MDL, FPE).

## 2.2 Non-Parametric Method — The Periodogram (Schuster, 1898)

The Periodogram is the **squared magnitude of the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)**, normalized by the number of samples $N$:

$$P_{xx}(f) = \frac{1}{N} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j 2\pi f n / f_s} \right|^2$$

In practice, computed efficiently via the **FFT**:

$$P_{xx}[k] = \frac{1}{N} \left| X[k] \right|^2, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

where $X[k] = \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variance Problem:** The variance of the periodogram **does not decrease with $N$** — it asymptotes to $\sigma^4$. Hence a single periodogram of a noisy ECG segment looks *“spiky”* and unreliable. This is why Welch's method is preferred in the lab.

## 2.3 Non-Parametric Method — Welch's Averaged Periodogram (1967)

Welch's improvement introduces three key modifications:

1. **Windowing** — Apply a window $w[n]$ (Hamming, Hann) to each segment to reduce leakage.
2. **Segmentation** — Split $x[n]$ into $K$ overlapping segments of length $L$: $x_i[n] = w[n] \cdot x[n + iD]$, where $D$ is the hop size (50% overlap is standard, i.e., $D = L/2$).
3. **Averaging** — Compute the periodogram of each segment, then average them.

$$P_{Welch}(f) = \frac{1}{K \cdot U} \sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_i[n] \, e^{-j 2\pi f n / f_s} \right|^2$$

where $U = \displaystyle\frac{1}{L} \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^2[n]$ is the **window energy normalization factor**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off:** Longer $L$ → better frequency resolution, more variance. Shorter $L$ → worse resolution, less variance. In ECG analysis, $L$ typically corresponds to **2.5–10 seconds** (to capture several heartbeats and HRV oscillations).

## 2.4 Parametric Method — Autoregressive (AR) Modeling

The **AR model of order $p$** assumes that the current sample $x[n]$ is a linear combination of its $p$ past samples plus a white noise innovation $e[n]$:

$$x[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, x[n-k] + e[n]$$

where:
- $a_k$ are the **AR coefficients** (model parameters)
- $e[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_e^2)$ is the **white noise** driving the model
- $p$ is the **model order**

The corresponding **PSD** is obtained by evaluating the transfer function of this all-pole filter on the unit circle:

$$P_{AR}(f) = \frac{\sigma_e^2}{\left\vert 1 + \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, e^{-j 2\pi f k / f_s} \right\vert^2}$$

### 2.4.1 Why AR for ECG Specifically?

The ECG is dominated by **sharp, resonant features** (QRS complexes, R-peaks) superimposed on a smoother baseline. These features are well-modeled by an **all-pole system** — just as the human vocal tract (which also has sharp resonances called formants) is modeled by an all-pole filter in speech processing. The AR model captures the **spectral peaks** of the ECG (QRS resonance) with very few parameters, often $p = 4$ to $p = 12$ is sufficient for a single beat.

### 2.4.2 Estimating AR Coefficients — The Yule-Walker Equations

Multiplying the AR model by $x[n-m]$ and taking the expectation yields the **Yule-Walker Normal Equations**:

$$\begin{aligned}
r_{xx}[m] &= -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, r_{xx}[m-k] + \sigma_e^2 \, \delta[m] \quad \text{for } m \geq 0
\end{aligned}$$

In matrix form, for $m = 1, 2, \ldots, p$:

$$\begin{bmatrix}
r[0] & r[1] & \cdots & r[p-1] \\
r[1] & r[0] & \cdots & r[p-2] \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
r[p-1] & r[p-2] & \cdots & r[0]
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_p
\end{bmatrix}
= -
\begin{bmatrix}
r[1] \\ r[2] \\ \vdots \\ r[p]
\end{bmatrix}$$

This is a **Toeplitz system** and is solved in $O(p^2)$ operations using the **Levinson-Durbin recursion** (essential in lab implementations).

### 2.4.3 Model Order Selection — AIC and MDL

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Choosing $p$ too low → *underfitting* (peaks missed, broad spectra). Choosing $p$ too high → *overfitting* (spurious peaks, spectral line splitting, unstable poles).

The two most common criteria are:

- **Akaike Information Criterion (AIC):**
$$AIC(p) = N \ln(\sigma_e^2) + 2p$$

- **Minimum Description Length (MDL):**
$$MDL(p) = N \ln(\sigma_e^2) + p \ln(N)$$

Choose the $p$ that **minimizes** the criterion. For ECG, $p \approx 4$ to $p \approx 10$ is typical.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Pin this to your lab record!**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Periodogram | $P_{xx}[k] = \frac{1}{N} \vert X[k] \vert^2$ | $X[k]$ = FFT, $N$ = samples |
| 2 | Welch's PSD | $P_W(f) = \frac{1}{K \cdot U} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \vert X_i(f) \vert^2$ | $K$ segments, $U$ = window energy |
| 3 | AR model (time domain) | $x[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x[n-k] + e[n]$ | $e[n] \sim$ white noise |
| 4 | AR model (PSD) | $P_{AR}(f) = \frac{\sigma_e^2}{\vert 1 + \sum a_k e^{-j2\pi fk/f_s} \vert^2}$ | All-pole spectrum |
| 5 | Yule-Walker (matrix) | $\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}$ | $\mathbf{R}$ is Toeplitz |
| 6 | Levinson-Durbin recursion | $a_{m,m} = -\frac{r[m] + \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_{m-1,k} r[m-k]}{\sigma_{e,m-1}^2}$ | Order-recursive |
| 7 | AIC | $AIC(p) = N \ln(\sigma_e^2) + 2p$ | Minimize over $p$ |
| 8 | MDL | $MDL(p) = N \ln(\sigma_e^2) + p \ln(N)$ | More conservative than AIC |
| 9 | Frequency resolution | $\Delta f = f_s / L$ | Welch: $L$ = segment length |
| 10 | HRV LF/HF Ratio | $LF/HF = \int_{0.04}^{0.15} P(f) df \,/\, \int_{0.15}^{0.4} P(f) df$ | Sympathovagal balance |
| 11 | Total Power (HRV) | $TP = \int_{0.0033}^{0.4} P(f) df$ | $ms^2$ when $P(f)$ in $s^2/Hz$ |
| 12 | Window energy (Hann) | $U = 0.375 L$ | $L$ = window length in samples |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does a Biomedical Engineer care about ECG spectra?**
>
> 1. **Holter Monitor Design** — Battery-powered 24-hour ECG recorders compress data using spectral features (dominant QRS frequency, HRV power bands) rather than storing every sample.
> 2. **Implantable Defibrillators (ICDs)** — Detect ventricular fibrillation (VF) by recognizing its characteristic **chaotic, broadband** spectrum (4–10 Hz with no dominant peak) versus normal sinus rhythm (sharp QRS peak at 10–15 Hz).
> 3. **Myocardial Ischemia Detection** — Reduced high-frequency (40–150 Hz) QRS components indicate conduction slowing due to ischemic tissue.
> 4. **Sleep Apnea & Autonomic Studies** — LF/HF ratio derived from HRV spectrum tracks sympathetic-parasympathetic balance.
> 5. **Biometric Authentication** — ECG spectral signatures are unique to individuals (cardiac geometry is personal).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From the AR Model Equation to the Power Spectrum

> **Goal:** Show rigorously why the AR model equation implies the PSD formula in Section 2.4.

### Step 1 — Rewrite the AR Difference Equation in the Z-Domain

Starting from the time-domain AR model:
$$x[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x[n-k] + e[n]$$

Take the **Z-transform** of both sides. Recall that $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n-k]\} = z^{-k} X(z)$ (time-shift property):

$$X(z) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, z^{-k} X(z) + E(z)$$

### Step 2 — Collect the $X(z)$ Terms

$$X(z) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k} X(z) = E(z)$$

$$X(z) \left[ 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k} \right] = E(z)$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Transfer Function $H(z)$

Define the **transfer function** $H(z) = \displaystyle\frac{X(z)}{E(z)}$:

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 + \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the key result: an AR model is equivalent to filtering white noise $e[n]$ through an **all-pole linear time-invariant filter** $H(z)$.

### Step 4 — Apply the Power Spectrum Identity

For an LTI system driven by white noise with variance $\sigma_e^2$, the output PSD equals the input PSD multiplied by $\vert H(f) \vert^2$:

$$P_{xx}(f) = \sigma_e^2 \cdot \left\vert H(f) \right\vert^2$$

Evaluate $H(z)$ on the unit circle by setting $z = e^{j 2\pi f / f_s}$:

$$H(f) = \frac{1}{1 + \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, e^{-j 2\pi f k / f_s}}$$

### Step 5 — Final PSD Expression

$$\boxed{P_{AR}(f) = \frac{\sigma_e^2}{\left\vert 1 + \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, e^{-j 2\pi f k / f_s} \right\vert^2}}$$

This is the **fundamental equation** that every AR-based ECG spectrum plot in your lab report is built upon. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Yule-Walker Equations from First Principles

### Step 1 — Multiply the AR Equation by $x[n-m]$ and Take Expectation

$$x[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x[n-k] + e[n]$$

Multiply both sides by $x[n-m]$ (for integer $m \geq 0$):

$$x[n] x[n-m] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x[n-k] x[n-m] + e[n] x[n-m]$$

### Step 2 — Take the Expectation Operator $E\{\cdot\}$

$$E\{x[n] x[n-m]\} = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k E\{x[n-k] x[n-m]\} + E\{e[n] x[n-m]\}$$

The term $E\{e[n] x[n-m]\} = 0$ for $m \geq 1$ because $e[n]$ is white noise and uncorrelated with past values of $x$.

### Step 3 — Recognize the Autocorrelation Function

By definition, $r_{xx}[m] = E\{x[n] x[n-m]\}$. Substituting:

$$r_{xx}[m] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, r_{xx}[m-k] \quad \text{for } m \geq 1$$

### Step 4 — Handle the Special Case $m = 0$

For $m = 0$, the term $E\{e[n] x[n]\} = \sigma_e^2$ (since $e[n]$ is white and present in $x[n]$). Hence:

$$r_{xx}[0] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, r_{xx}[k] + \sigma_e^2$$

This is the **innovation variance** relation. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — AR(2) Spectrum of an ECG Beat

> **Problem:** Suppose a single ECG beat is modeled as an AR(2) process with coefficients $a_1 = -1.5$, $a_2 = 0.7$, and innovation variance $\sigma_e^2 = 0.05$. The sampling rate is $f_s = 250$ Hz. Plot the PSD from $f = 0$ to $f = 60$ Hz.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Write the denominator polynomial.**

$$D(f) = 1 + a_1 e^{-j 2\pi f / f_s} + a_2 e^{-j 4\pi f / f_s}$$

Substituting coefficients:
$$D(f) = 1 - 1.5 \, e^{-j 2\pi f / 250} + 0.7 \, e^{-j 4\pi f / 250}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\vert D(f) \vert^2$ at a representative frequency, say $f = 12$ Hz.**

Compute the complex exponentials:
$$e^{-j 2\pi \cdot 12 / 250} = e^{-j 0.3016} \approx \cos(0.3016) - j\sin(0.3016) \approx 0.9548 - j \, 0.2973$$

$$e^{-j 4\pi \cdot 12 / 250} = e^{-j 0.6032} \approx 0.9119 - j \, 0.5681$$

Now substitute:

$$D(12) = 1 - 1.5(0.9548 - j0.2973) + 0.7(0.9119 - j0.5681)$$

$$= 1 - 1.4322 + j0.4460 + 0.6383 - j0.3977$$

$$= (1 - 1.4322 + 0.6383) + j(0.4460 - 0.3977)$$

$$= 0.2061 + j0.0483$$

Therefore:
$$\vert D(12) \vert^2 = (0.2061)^2 + (0.0483)^2 = 0.04247 + 0.00233 = 0.04480$$

**Step 3: Compute the PSD value at $f = 12$ Hz.**

$$P_{AR}(12) = \frac{0.05}{0.04480} \approx 1.116 \text{ (arbitrary units)}$$

**Step 4: Repeat for a range of frequencies and plot.**

Repeating for $f = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, 60$ Hz (which is the standard practice in MATLAB's `pyulear` or `pburg` functions), we obtain:

| Frequency (Hz) | $P_{AR}(f)$ (a.u.) |
|----------------|--------------------|
| 0 | 0.043 |
| 5 | 0.187 |
| 10 | 0.812 |
| **12** | **1.116** |
| 15 | 0.945 |
| 20 | 0.412 |
| 30 | 0.156 |
| 40 | 0.082 |
| 50 | 0.054 |
| 60 | 0.041 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The PSD peaks at **$f \approx 12$ Hz**, which corresponds to the **dominant QRS resonance** — exactly the expected clinical finding for a normal ECG. The peak is sharp and well-resolved, demonstrating the **superior frequency resolution of the parametric AR method** compared to a raw periodogram on a short segment.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this decision matrix during your lab viva when the examiner asks "Why did you choose Welch over AR?"**

| Criterion | Periodogram | Welch's Method | AR (Parametric) |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Segment length needed** | Long ($N > 1024$ recommended) | Medium (256–512) | **Short** (64–256) |
| **Frequency resolution** | High (if $N$ large) | Moderate | **Highest** |
| **Spectral variance** | High (unusable for short) | Reduced by averaging | **Lowest** |
| **Spectral leakage** | Severe | Reduced (windowing) | **Inherently none** |
| **Computational cost** | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(KL \log L)$ | $O(p^2)$ via Levinson-Durbin |
| **Assumes a model?** | No | No | **Yes (linear, all-pole)** |
| **Model order selection** | Not needed | Not needed | **Required (AIC/MDL)** |
| **Best ECG use-case** | Educational only | **Routine HRV analysis** | **Short beats, late potentials, VF detection** |
| **MATLAB function** | `periodogram(x)` | `pwelch(x, win, noverlap)` | `pyulear(x, p, Nfft)` or `arburg(x, p)` |

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ECG Spectral Analysis Pipeline

The following Mermaid flowchart represents the complete experimental pipeline a KTU student would implement in MATLAB/Octave for Module Experiment 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Experiment] --> B[Acquire or Load ECG Signal]
    B --> C{Is Signal Clean?}
    C -- No --> D[Apply Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 40 Hz]
    D --> E[Apply Notch Filter 50 Hz]
    E --> F[Cleaned ECG Signal]
    C -- Yes --> F
    F --> G[Segment the Signal]
    G --> H[Choose Segment Length N]
    H --> I[Method 1 Non Parametric Periodogram]
    H --> J[Method 2 Non Parametric Welch Method]
    H --> K[Method 3 Parametric AR Modeling]
    I --> L[Compute FFT and Squared Magnitude]
    J --> M[Split into K Overlapping Segments]
    M --> N[Window each Segment Hann]
    N --> O[FFT each Segment]
    O --> P[Average Periodograms]
    K --> Q[Select Model Order via AIC and MDL]
    Q --> R[Estimate AR Coefficients using Levinson Durbin]
    R --> S[Compute All Pole PSD]
    L --> T[Plot PSD in dB vs Frequency Hz]
    P --> T
    S --> T
    T --> U{Interpret Spectrum}
    U --> V[Identify QRS Peak 10 to 15 Hz]
    U --> W[Check HF QRS 40 to 150 Hz for Ischemia]
    U --> X[Compute LF HF Ratio for HRV]
    V --> Y[Document Findings in Lab Record]
    W --> Y
    X --> Y
    Y --> Z[End Experiment]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram in a viva:** Trace the path of an ECG sample from the input file to the final PSD plot, mentioning the specific MATLAB function used at each block (e.g., `butter`, `iirnotch`, `pwelch`, `pyulear`, `arburg`).

## 4.3 Block Diagram — The AR Spectral Estimator Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input ECG Segment xn] --> B[Compute Autocorrelation rxx]
    B --> C[Form Toeplitz Matrix R]
    C --> D[Levinson Durbin Recursion]
    D --> E[AR Coefficients a1 to ap]
    D --> F[Innovation Variance sigma squared]
    E --> G[Form Denominator Polynomial 1 plus sum ak z to power minus k]
    F --> H[Numerator sigma squared]
    G --> I[Evaluate on Unit Circle z equals exp j 2 pi f by fs]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute Magnitude Squared]
    J --> K[Output PSD P AR of f]
    L[Model Order Selection AIC and MDL] --> D
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading guide for the examiner:** The autocorrelation step is the bridge between the *time-domain samples* and the *frequency-domain model*. The Toeplitz structure is what makes the Levinson-Durbin recursion possible in $O(p^2)$ time, making AR estimation computationally attractive for real-time ECG analysis on embedded hardware (e.g., wearable Holter monitors).
