# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the IIR Notch Filter Transfer Function

We begin by placing zeros on the unit circle at the conjugate pair corresponding to $\pm\omega_0$:

**Step 1 — Define the zeros.**
The zeros are at $z = e^{\pm j\omega_0}$. Their polynomial is:

$$
(z - e^{j\omega_0})(z - e^{-j\omega_0}) \;=\; z^2 - (e^{j\omega_0} + e^{-j\omega_0}) z + 1 \;=\; z^2 - 2\cos\omega_0 \, z + 1
$$

Converting to $z^{-1}$ form (multiply by $z^{-2}$):

$$
\text{Zero polynomial} \;=\; 1 - 2\cos\omega_0 \, z^{-1} + z^{-2}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the poles.**
Place poles at the same angle $\pm\omega_0$ but at a radial distance $r < 1$ (slightly *inside* the unit circle). The pole polynomial is:

$$
(z - re^{j\omega_0})(z - re^{-j\omega_0}) \;=\; z^2 - 2r\cos\omega_0 \, z + r^2
$$

In $z^{-1}$ form:

$$
\text{Pole polynomial} \;=\; 1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 \, z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the transfer function.**

$$
H(z) \;=\; \frac{\text{Zero polynomial}}{\text{Pole polynomial}} \;=\; \frac{1 - 2\cos\omega_0 \, z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 \, z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the pole radius in terms of -3 dB bandwidth.**
For a *narrow* notch, the half-power ($-3$ dB) frequencies $\omega_0 \pm B/2$ satisfy $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 = 1/2$. Linearizing near $\omega_0$ gives the well-known engineering approximation:

$$
r \;\approx\; 1 - \frac{\pi B}{f_s}
$$

This is the canonical design equation used in MATLAB's `iirnotch` function.

**Step 5 — Coefficient extraction.**

$$
b_0 = 1, \quad b_1 = -2\cos\omega_0, \quad b_2 = 1
$$

$$
a_0 = 1, \quad a_1 = -2r\cos\omega_0, \quad a_2 = r^2
$$

**Step 6 — Implement as a difference equation.**

$$
y[n] \;=\; b_0 x[n] + b_1 x[n-1] + b_2 x[n-2] - a_1 y[n-1] - a_2 y[n-2]
$$

$$
\boxed{\,y[n] \;=\; x[n] - 2\cos\omega_0 \, x[n-1] + x[n-2] + 2r\cos\omega_0 \, y[n-1] - r^2 y[n-2]\,}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 50 Hz Notch at $f_s = 500$ Hz

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a KTU 2024 favourite — examiners expect a fully worked numerical for a 7-mark sub-question.**

**Given:**
- Mains frequency $f_0 = 50$ Hz
- Sampling rate $f_s = 500$ Hz
- Desired -3 dB bandwidth $B = 4$ Hz
- Required: IIR notch filter coefficients

**Step 1 — Normalized notch frequency.**

$$
\omega_0 \;=\; \frac{2\pi f_0}{f_s} \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 50}{500} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{5} \;=\; 0.2\pi \text{ rad/sample}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\cos\omega_0$.**

$$
\cos(0.2\pi) \;=\; \cos(36^\circ) \;=\; 0.8090
$$

**Step 3 — Compute pole radius $r$.**

$$
r \;=\; 1 - \frac{\pi B}{f_s} \;=\; 1 - \frac{\pi \times 4}{500} \;=\; 1 - 0.02513 \;=\; 0.9749
$$

**Step 4 — Quality factor check.**

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{f_0}{B} \;=\; \frac{50}{4} \;=\; 12.5
$$

> This is on the *lower* side; a $Q$ of 30–50 (i.e., $B \approx 1$–1.7 Hz) is preferred for ECG to avoid distorting the QRS.

**Step 5 — Compute the feedforward coefficients.**

$$
b_0 = 1
$$

$$
b_1 = -2\cos\omega_0 = -2 \times 0.8090 = -1.6180
$$

$$
b_2 = 1
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the feedback coefficients.**

$$
a_1 = -2r\cos\omega_0 = -2 \times 0.9749 \times 0.8090 = -1.5773
$$

$$
a_2 = r^2 = 0.9749^2 = 0.9504
$$

**Step 7 — Final difference equation.**

$$
y[n] \;=\; x[n] - 1.6180\,x[n-1] + x[n-2] + 1.5773\,y[n-1] - 0.9504\,y[n-2]
$$

**Step 8 — Verification: magnitude at $\omega = \omega_0$.**

Substitute $z = e^{j\omega_0}$ into $H(z)$. The numerator becomes $1 - 2\cos\omega_0 \cdot e^{-j\omega_0} + e^{-j2\omega_0}$. Using $e^{-j\omega_0} = \cos\omega_0 - j\sin\omega_0$:

$$
\text{Num} \;=\; 1 - 2\cos\omega_0(\cos\omega_0 - j\sin\omega_0) + (\cos 2\omega_0 - j\sin 2\omega_0)
$$

$$
= \;1 - 2\cos^2\omega_0 + j 2\cos\omega_0 \sin\omega_0 + \cos 2\omega_0 - j\sin 2\omega_0
$$

Using $1 - 2\cos^2\omega_0 = -\cos 2\omega_0$:

$$
\text{Num} \;=\; -\cos 2\omega_0 + \cos 2\omega_0 + j(2\cos\omega_0 \sin\omega_0 - \sin 2\omega_0) \;=\; 0
$$

The numerator is **exactly zero** at $z = e^{j\omega_0}$, confirming the notch depth. The poles at $re^{\pm j\omega_0}$ are strictly *inside* the unit circle ($r = 0.9749 < 1$), ensuring **BIBO stability**.

## 3.3 Derivation of the LMS Adaptive Filter Update

The adaptive filter for PLI rejection uses a two-input architecture:
- **Primary input** $d[n]$: the noisy ECG (target = clean ECG).
- **Reference input** $x[n]$: a locally generated pure 50 Hz sinusoid: $x[n] = \sin(2\pi f_0 n T_s)$.

A two-weight adaptive linear combiner is used:

$$
y[n] \;=\; w_0[n]\,x[n] + w_1[n]\,x[n-1] \;=\; \mathbf{w}^T[n] \,\mathbf{x}[n]
$$

**Step 1 — Define the error signal (clean ECG estimate).**

$$
e[n] \;=\; d[n] - y[n]
$$

**Step 2 — Define the instantaneous MSE cost function.**

$$
J[n] \;=\; \frac{1}{2} e^2[n] \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\bigl(d[n] - \mathbf{w}^T[n]\mathbf{x}[n]\bigr)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Take the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{w}$.**

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J[n] \;=\; -e[n]\,\mathbf{x}[n]
$$

**Step 4 — Apply steepest descent (LMS update rule).**

$$
\mathbf{w}[n+1] \;=\; \mathbf{w}[n] - \mu \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J[n] \;=\; \mathbf{w}[n] + 2\mu\, e[n]\,\mathbf{x}[n]
$$

**Step 5 — Stability condition.**
For convergence in the mean, the step size must satisfy:

$$
0 \;<\; \mu \;<\; \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}
$$

where $\lambda_{max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of the input autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R} = E\{\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^T\}$.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — IIR Notch vs LMS Adaptive

| Criterion | IIR Notch | LMS Adaptive |
|---|---|---|
| Needs reference input? | No | Yes (50 Hz reference) |
| Handles frequency drift of mains? | No (fixed $f_0$) | Yes — tracks drift |
| Computational cost (per sample) | 4 mults + 4 adds | 2N + 1 mults (N = filter length) |
| Transient response | Instant (recursive) | Converges in $O(1/\mu)$ samples |
| Phase distortion | Non-linear phase (causes ringing) | Linear if FIR-structure adaptive filter used |
| Best use case | Fixed-frequency clean mains | Variable-frequency / portable equipment |

flowchart LR

    hp --> adc[ADC at fs]

```

flowchart TB
    origin((Origin z=0))
    z1[/ Zero at z = e to power j omega 0 /]
    z2[\ Zero at z = e to power -j omega 0 \]

    p2([ Pole at z = r e to power -j omega 0 ])
    origin --- z1
    origin --- z2
    origin --- p1
    origin --- p2
    annot[Zeros EXACTLY on unit circle create infinite attenuation. Poles INSIDE the unit circle at radius r control the notch sharpness and guarantee BIBO stability. Pole radius r equals 1 minus pi B over f s.]
    annot --- p1
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sinref[Oscillator: 50 Hz Sine] --> refbuf[Reference Buffer]
    refbuf --> adf[Adaptive FIR Filter w0, w1]
    ecg[Primary Input: ECG plus 50 Hz PLI] --> sum[Subtractor]
    adf --> y[Estimated PLI y of n]
    y --> sum
    sum --> e[Error e of n = Clean ECG Estimate]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question] — 3 Marks

**What is powerline interference in ECG? List any two sources of its origin.**

**Model Answer:**
Powerline interference (PLI) is a **deterministic sinusoidal noise at the AC mains frequency (50 Hz in India, 60 Hz in the USA)** superimposed on the ECG signal, often accompanied by odd harmonics (100 Hz, 150 Hz). It degrades the Signal-to-Noise Ratio and can mask diagnostic features like the ST segment.

**Two sources:**
1. **Electromagnetic (inductive) coupling** — Time-varying magnetic fields from nearby power cables induce an EMF in the lead wires.
2. **Capacitive (electrostatic) coupling** — Stray capacitance between power lines and ECG electrodes injects displacement current.
3. *(Valid alternative: Ground-loop conduction between equipment chassis and patient earth.)*

**Marks split (as per KTU pattern):** Definition 1.5 M + Sources 1 × 1.5 M = 3 M.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question] — 3 Marks

**Define a notch filter. Why is it preferred over a low-pass filter for PLI removal in ECG?**

**Model Answer:**
A **notch filter** (band-stop filter) is a second-order filter that produces a *very narrow* and *very deep* null at a single specified frequency $f_0$ while passing all other frequencies with unit gain.

**Why preferred over LPF:**
- A low-pass filter would attenuate frequencies above $f_0$ indiscriminately, destroying high-frequency QRS components and EMG information.
- The ECG energy extends up to ~150 Hz, and a notch removes **only** the offending 50 Hz (or 100 Hz, 150 Hz harmonics) with minimal collateral damage.
- A notch preserves the diagnostic morphology of the QRS, ST, and T waves.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Essay Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ THIS CAREFULLY**
>
> 1. **Coefficient-sign error:** Many students write $a_1 = +2r\cos\omega_0$ instead of $a_1 = -2r\cos\omega_0$. This produces a *high-pass* filter, not a notch. **Always double-check the sign from the difference equation.**
> 2. **Confusing $\omega$ with $f$:** Substituting $f_0 = 50$ directly into a cosine (without normalising by $f_s$) gives $\cos(50) = 0.965$, which is **wrong** by orders of magnitude. Always compute $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 / f_s$ first.
> 3. **Forgetting the stability condition $r < 1$:** If a student writes $r > 1$ in the denominator polynomial, the filter is unstable and the output diverges. Examiners deduct full marks.
> 4. **Ignoring phase linearity:** A 14-mark answer that does *not* discuss the non-linear phase of an IIR notch and its effect on QRS morphology is considered incomplete.
> 5. **No plot, no credit:** In KTU lab records, a filter is *not* considered "applied" unless you show both the time-domain and the frequency-domain (FFT) plots before and after filtering.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — IIR Notch Filter Design

**`[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper Pattern]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a complete ECG acquisition system. Identify where in the chain powerline interference enters. — **7 Marks**

**Model Solution (a):**

A standard ECG acquisition chain consists of:

| Stage | Function |
|---|---|
| 1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl)** | Transduce ionic skin currents into electronic voltages. |
| 2. **Lead wires (shielded)** | Carry the differential bioelectric potential. |
| 3. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** | High-CMRR differential amplification; rejects common-mode PLI. |
| 4. **Driven Right Leg (DRL)** | Active common-mode feedback that drives the patient body to the common-mode voltage, drastically reducing 50 Hz pickup. |
| 5. **Analog HPF (0.05 Hz)** | Blocks DC electrode offset. |
| 6. **Analog LPF (~150 Hz anti-alias)** | Prevents aliasing before sampling. |
| 7. **ADC** | Digitises the analog ECG. |
| 8. **Digital Notch filter (DSP)** | Final software-based 50 Hz removal. |
| 9. **Display / Storage** | Monitor / MIT-BIH record. |

**Where PLI enters:**
- Inductively at the **lead wire** stage (Stage 2).
- Capacitively between the power line and the patient body.
- Conductively via the **ground loop** if chassis earth ≠ patient earth.

The notch filter is applied at the digital stage (8) to provide final, sharp, software-based 50 Hz attenuation.

**(b)** Design a second-order IIR notch filter to remove 50 Hz interference from an ECG sampled at $f_s = 250$ Hz. Take the -3 dB bandwidth as $B = 2$ Hz. Compute all five filter coefficients, write the difference equation, and plot the pole-zero diagram. — **7 Marks**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Normalised frequency.**

$$
\omega_0 \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 50}{250} \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{5} \;=\; 0.4\pi \text{ rad/sample} \;=\; 72^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — $\cos\omega_0$.**

$$
\cos(0.4\pi) \;=\; \cos(72^\circ) \;=\; 0.3090
$$

**Step 3 — Quality factor.**

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{f_0}{B} \;=\; \frac{50}{2} \;=\; 25
$$

**Step 4 — Pole radius.**

$$
r \;=\; 1 - \frac{\pi B}{f_s} \;=\; 1 - \frac{\pi \times 2}{250} \;=\; 1 - 0.02513 \;=\; 0.9749
$$

**Step 5 — Coefficients.**

$$
b_0 = 1
$$

$$
b_1 = -2\cos\omega_0 = -2(0.3090) = -0.6180
$$

$$
b_2 = 1
$$

$$
a_1 = -2r\cos\omega_0 = -2(0.9749)(0.3090) = -0.6025
$$

$$
a_2 = r^2 = (0.9749)^2 = 0.9504
$$

**Step 6 — Difference equation.**

$$
y[n] \;=\; x[n] - 0.6180\,x[n-1] + x[n-2] + 0.6025\,y[n-1] - 0.9504\,y[n-2]
$$

**Step 7 — Pole-zero diagram (description for board exam):**

- Two **zeros** on the unit circle at angles $+0.4\pi$ and $-0.4\pi$ rad.
- Two **poles** at the same angles but at radius $r = 0.9749$ (just inside the unit circle).
- The notch depth is theoretically infinite; the -3 dB bandwidth is 2 Hz.

**Step 8 — Frequency response verification:**
At $\omega = 0.4\pi$ the numerator is exactly zero, giving **infinite attenuation** at 50 Hz. The filter is **BIBO stable** since $r < 1$.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Adaptive LMS Filter

**`[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper Pattern]`**

**(a)** Explain the principle of operation of an LMS adaptive filter for powerline interference rejection. Derive the weight update equation. — **7 Marks**

**Model Solution (a):**

The LMS adaptive filter is a **self-adjusting linear combiner** that learns the optimal weights to model the PLI sinusoid and subtract it from the primary input. Its architecture has two inputs:

- **Primary input $d[n]$** = the noisy ECG (clean ECG + 50 Hz interference).
- **Reference input $x[n]$** = a pure 50 Hz sinusoid generated locally (often from the same mains frequency via a Phase-Locked Loop).

The filter output (an estimate of the PLI) is:

$$
y[n] \;=\; \mathbf{w}^T[n]\,\mathbf{x}[n] \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k[n]\,x[n-k]
$$

The **error signal**, which is the clean ECG estimate, is:

$$
e[n] \;=\; d[n] - y[n]
$$

The instantaneous cost function is:

$$
J[n] \;=\; \frac{1}{2}e^2[n]
$$

Taking the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{w}$:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}J[n] \;=\; -e[n]\,\mathbf{x}[n]
$$

Applying the steepest-descent rule:

$$
\boxed{\,\mathbf{w}[n+1] \;=\; \mathbf{w}[n] + 2\mu\,e[n]\,\mathbf{x}[n]\,}
$$

where $\mu$ is the step-size. The filter converges in the mean when $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{max}$.

**(b)** Compare the LMS adaptive filter with the IIR notch filter for ECG PLI rejection on the basis of (i) ability to track mains frequency drift, (ii) phase distortion, (iii) computational complexity, and (iv) reference signal requirement. — **7 Marks**

**Model Solution (b):**

| Criterion | IIR Notch Filter | LMS Adaptive Filter |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Frequency-drift tracking | **Poor** — fixed $f_0$; needs redesign for any drift. | **Excellent** — adapts to slow drift in real time. |
| (ii) Phase distortion | **Non-linear phase** — causes ringing near QRS. | **Linear phase** if adaptive filter is FIR-structured. |
| (iii) Computational load | **Very low** — 4 mults + 4 adds per sample. | **Higher** — $2N+1$ mults per sample; for $N=2$, 5 mults. |
| (iv) Reference input | **Not required** — operates on single input. | **Required** — needs a clean 50 Hz reference (PLL or internal oscillator). |
| (v) Convergence / transient | **Instant** — recursive with no learning. | **Adaptive** — requires $\approx 1/\mu$ samples to converge. |
| (vi) Robustness to PLI amplitude changes | **Robust** — gain at 50 Hz is 0 regardless. | **Robust** — weights track amplitude automatically. |
| (vii) Best use case | Fixed $f_0$ mains, bench-top ECG | Portable, battery-powered, variable-mains instruments |

A 7-mark answer should discuss at least **4** of the 7 rows above with clear, contrasting statements.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this on the morning of the exam.**

- ✅ **PLI = 50 Hz (India) or 60 Hz (USA)** sinusoidal noise + odd harmonics.
- ✅ **Sources** = inductive, capacitive, conductive (ground loop), common-mode.
- ✅ **Two software fixes** = (1) IIR notch, (2) LMS adaptive filter.
- ✅ **IIR Notch $H(z)$** has zeros on the unit circle at $\pm \omega_0$ and poles just inside at radius $r$.
- ✅ **$\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 / f_s$** — *always* normalise the frequency.
- ✅ **$r = 1 - \pi B / f_s$** — pole radius sets the notch width.
- ✅ **$Q = f_0 / B$** — quality factor (use 30–50 for ECG).
- ✅ **Coefficients:** $b = [1, -2\cos\omega_0, 1]$ and $a = [1, -2r\cos\omega_0, r^2]$.
- ✅ **Stability requires $r < 1$** — guaranteed if the formula above is used correctly.
- ✅ **LMS update:** $\mathbf{w}[n+1] = \mathbf{w}[n] + 2\mu\,e[n]\,\mathbf{x}[n]$.
- ✅ **LMS stability:** $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{max}$.
- ✅ **Hardware first, software last** — proper shielding, DRL, and high-CMRR INA reduce the interference *before* digital filtering.
- ✅ **Always present time-domain + frequency-domain plots** in the KTU lab record.
- ✅ **Threshold:** residual PLI should be **< 5 µV peak-to-peak**.
- ✅ **Trade-off:** narrow notch ($Q$ large) preserves QRS; wide notch ($Q$ small) distorts QRS but removes more noise.
- ✅ **ECE vs Mains frequency confusion:** Indian mains is **50 Hz**; do not write 60 Hz in an Indian KTU exam.
- ✅ **Common error:** do not forget to apply the notch *after* baseline wander removal (HPF 0.5 Hz) but *before* R-peak detection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arrhythmia analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL507 - PCBRL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Arrhythmia analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Arrhythmia Analysis** is the systematic detection, characterization, and classification of irregularities in the cardiac rhythm by processing the electrocardiogram (ECG) signal. An **arrhythmia** (also called *dysrhythmia*) is defined as any deviation from the normal sinus rhythm, where the sinoatrial (SA) node initiates each heartbeat at a regular rate between **60–100 beats per minute (bpm)** in a resting adult.

In the context of **PCBRL507 (Biomedical Signal Processing / Medical Electronics Laboratory)**, Module Experiment 2 deals with the practical acquisition, conditioning, feature extraction, and classification of arrhythmic ECG beats using digital signal processing techniques.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Students are expected to implement the signal conditioning chain — filtering (notch, band-pass, low-pass), baseline wander removal, R-peak detection, R–R interval computation, and classification of at least three arrhythmia classes (e.g., Normal, PVC, AF).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your heart as a **marching band** of muscle cells. The **SA node** is the **drummer** who sets the pace — every cell follows the drummer's beat. An ECG is like a **microphone recording** of the drummer's tapping pattern.

- **Normal Sinus Rhythm** → the drummer taps evenly, like a metronome: *tap… tap… tap… tap…* with a constant gap.
- **Tachycardia** → the drummer rushes: *tap tap tap tap tap* (too fast, >100 bpm).
- **Bradycardia** → the drummer is sleepy: *tap……… tap……… tap* (too slow, <60 bpm).
- **Atrial Fibrillation** → the drummer is drunk and the rest of the band members are tapping at random — chaotic, no clear pattern.
- **Premature Ventricular Contraction (PVC)** → a *rogue bass drummer* fires a loud beat out of turn, disrupting the rhythm.
- **Heart Block** → the drummer's signal is **delayed or blocked** from reaching the rest of the band, so some beats are skipped or late.

**Arrhythmia analysis** is therefore the art of *listening to this ECG microphone recording* and identifying *what kind of disturbance* is happening in the band.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (must be memorized):**
> - **Normal Heart Rate (HR): 60–100 bpm**
> - **QRS duration: 0.06–0.10 s**
> - **PR interval: 0.12–0.20 s**
> - **QT interval: 0.36–0.44 s**
> - **Sampling rate (clinical ECG): ≥ 500 Hz**

---

## 1.3 Clinical Significance

Arrhythmia analysis is the foundation of:
- **Holter monitoring** (24-hr ambulatory ECG)
- **Intensive Care Unit (ICU) patient monitoring**
- **Automated External Defibrillators (AED)**
- **Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillators (ICD)**
- **AI-based cardiac diagnosis (deep learning ECG)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Untreated ventricular fibrillation (VF)** causes death within **4–6 minutes** due to lack of cardiac output. This is why automated arrhythmia detection algorithms are life-critical in bedside monitors and AEDs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cardiac Conduction System (Foundation Block)

The ECG is the *electrical footprint* of the cardiac conduction pathway. Memorize this sequence:

| Step | Structure | Function | ECG Feature |
|------|-----------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **SA Node** | Natural pacemaker (60–100 bpm) | Initiates P wave |
| 2 | **Atrial Conduction** | Depolarization of atria | **P wave** (0.08–0.10 s, < 2.5 mm) |
| 3 | **AV Node Delay** | ~0.1 s delay for ventricular filling | **PR interval / segment** |
| 4 | **Bundle of His → Bundle Branches → Purkinje Fibers** | Rapid ventricular depolarization | **QRS complex** (0.06–0.10 s) |
| 5 | **Ventricular Repolarization** | Recovery of ventricles | **T wave** (asymmetric, ~0.16 s) |
| 6 | **Papillary Muscle Repolarization** (optional) | Sometimes visible | **U wave** |

---

## 2.2 Classification of Major Arrhythmias (KTU High-Yield)

### A. Supraventricular Arrhythmias (Origin: Above the ventricles)
1. **Sinus Tachycardia** — HR > 100 bpm, regular, normal P wave, narrow QRS.
2. **Sinus Bradycardia** — HR < 60 bpm, regular, normal P wave, narrow QRS.
3. **Atrial Fibrillation (AF)** — Irregularly irregular, *no discrete P wave*, chaotic baseline (fibrillatory waves), narrow QRS.
4. **Atrial Flutter** — Regular, sawtooth flutter waves (F waves) at ~300 bpm, with 2:1, 3:1, or 4:1 AV conduction.
5. **Premature Atrial Contraction (PAC)** — Early beat with abnormal P wave, narrow QRS, followed by a compensatory pause.

### B. Ventricular Arrhythmias (Origin: Ventricles — life-threatening)
6. **Premature Ventricular Contraction (PVC)** — Wide, bizarre QRS (>0.12 s), no preceding P wave, compensatory pause.
7. **Ventricular Tachycardia (VT)** — ≥ 3 consecutive PVCs, rate 100–250 bpm, wide QRS.
8. **Ventricular Fibrillation (VF)** — Chaotic, no identifiable QRS, no effective cardiac output → **cardiac arrest**.

### C. Conduction Blocks
9. **First-Degree AV Block** — PR interval > 0.20 s, every P followed by QRS.
10. **Second-Degree AV Block, Mobitz Type I (Wenckebach)** — Progressive PR lengthening until a QRS is dropped.
11. **Second-Degree AV Block, Mobitz Type II** — Constant PR with sudden dropped QRS.
12. **Third-Degree (Complete) AV Block** — Complete AV dissociation, P and QRS march independently.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Lifesaver)

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` in equations below to keep LaTeX compatibility inside the table.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Normal Value | Units |
|---|-----------|----------------------|--------------|-------|
| 1 | **Heart Rate (paper ECG, 25 mm/s)** | $HR = \dfrac{1500}{N_{small\ boxes}}$ or $\dfrac{300}{N_{large\ boxes}}$ | 60 – 100 | bpm |
| 2 | **Heart Rate (digital, R–R interval)** | $HR = \dfrac{60}{T_{R-R}}$ | 60 – 100 | bpm |
| 3 | **R–R Interval** | $T_{R-R} = \dfrac{60}{HR}$ | 0.6 – 1.0 | s |
| 4 | **PR Interval** | Start of P to start of QRS | 0.12 – 0.20 | s |
| 5 | **QRS Duration** | Start of Q to end of S | 0.06 – 0.10 | s |
| 6 | **QT Interval** | Start of Q to end of T | 0.36 – 0.44 | s |
| 7 | **Corrected QT (Bazett's)** | $QT_c = \dfrac{QT}{\sqrt{RR}}$ | < 0.44 (men), < 0.46 (women) | s |
| 8 | **SDNN (HRV time-domain)** | $SDNN = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(RR_i - \overline{RR})^2}$ | 141 ± 39 (young adults) | ms |
| 9 | **RMSSD (HRV short-term)** | $RMSSD = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1}(RR_{i+1}-RR_i)^2}$ | 27 ± 12 | ms |
| 10 | **pNN50** | $pNN50 = \dfrac{\vert NN_{50} \vert}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Varies | % |
| 11 | **LF Power Band** | Spectral power in 0.04–0.15 Hz | — | ms² |
| 12 | **HF Power Band** | Spectral power in 0.15–0.40 Hz (parasympathetic) | — | ms² |
| 13 | **LF/HF Ratio** | Sympathovagal balance | 1–2 | dimensionless |
| 14 | **Sampling Rate (Nyquist)** | $f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max}$ ; for ECG $f_{max} \approx 150$ Hz → $f_s \geq 500$ Hz | ≥ 500 | Hz |
| 15 | **Pan-Tompkins Threshold** | Adaptive: $THR_{i+1} = 0.7 \cdot THR_i + 0.3 \cdot PEAK_i$ | — | — |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wearable ECG patches (Apple Watch, Fitbit)** use these algorithms in real time on low-power DSP chips.
- **ICU bedside monitors** sample ECG at 500–1000 Hz and run **R-peak detection + HRV analysis** continuously.
- **Ambulatory Holter recorders** store 24 hours of ECG, then offline analysis detects ectopic beats (PAC, PVC).
- **AI/ML pipelines** (CNNs, LSTMs) trained on the **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database** (48 records, 360 Hz) use these features as inputs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database** is the *de-facto* benchmark dataset for any ECG classification algorithm. Every KTU project that touches arrhythmia should ideally reference it.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Heart Rate from R–R Interval

**Problem:** Given two consecutive R-peaks at samples $n_1$ and $n_2$ with sampling frequency $f_s$, derive the heart rate in bpm.

**Step 1:** Compute the number of samples between R-peaks:

$$
\Delta n = n_2 - n_1
$$

**Step 2:** Convert sample count to time (seconds):

$$
T_{R-R} = \dfrac{\Delta n}{f_s} \quad [\text{seconds}]
$$

**Step 3:** Convert period to frequency (beats per second):

$$
f_{beat} = \dfrac{1}{T_{R-R}} = \dfrac{f_s}{\Delta n} \quad [\text{Hz}]
$$

**Step 4:** Convert Hz to beats per minute (1 Hz = 60 bpm):

$$
\boxed{HR = 60 \cdot \dfrac{f_s}{n_2 - n_1} \quad [\text{bpm}]}
$$

**Numerical Example:** $f_s = 500$ Hz, $n_2 - n_1 = 420$ samples.

$$
T_{R-R} = \dfrac{420}{500} = 0.84 \text{ s}
$$

$$
HR = \dfrac{60}{0.84} = 71.43 \text{ bpm}
$$

This value lies within the **normal sinus rhythm** range of 60–100 bpm. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — SDNN (Standard Deviation of NN Intervals)

**Problem:** Show that SDNN is a direct measure of overall HRV.

**Step 1:** Collect $N$ successive R–R intervals (called **NN intervals** if both are normal sinus beats):

$$
\{NN_1, NN_2, \ldots, NN_N\}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the mean:

$$
\overline{NN} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} NN_i
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the deviation of each NN from the mean:

$$
d_i = NN_i - \overline{NN}
$$

**Step 4:** Square each deviation (removes sign, penalizes large deviations):

$$
d_i^2 = (NN_i - \overline{NN})^2
$$

**Step 5:** Sum the squared deviations and divide by $(N-1)$ (Bessel's correction for unbiased estimator):

$$
s^2 = \dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (NN_i - \overline{NN})^2
$$

**Step 6:** Take the square root to return to the original units (ms):

$$
\boxed{SDNN = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (NN_i - \overline{NN})^2}}
$$

**Why $(N-1)$ and not $N$?** Because the mean $\overline{NN}$ was estimated from the same data, we lose **1 degree of freedom**. Bessel's correction restores the **unbiased** property of the variance estimator.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — RMSSD (Root Mean Square of Successive Differences)

**Step 1:** Compute successive differences of NN intervals:

$$
\Delta NN_i = NN_{i+1} - NN_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N-1
$$

**Step 2:** Square each successive difference:

$$
(\Delta NN_i)^2
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the mean of the squared differences:

$$
\overline{(\Delta NN)^2} = \dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} (NN_{i+1} - NN_i)^2
$$

**Step 4:** Take the square root:

$$
\boxed{RMSSD = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} (NN_{i+1} - NN_i)^2}}
$$

**Interpretation:** RMSSD quantifies *beat-to-beat variability* and is dominated by **parasympathetic (vagal) activity** — the high-frequency component of HRV. High RMSSD = healthy autonomic regulation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Pan-Tompkins QRS Detection Algorithm (Simplified Pipeline)

The Pan-Tompkins algorithm (1985) is the **gold standard** for real-time R-peak detection. The complete signal chain:

**Stage 1: Band-pass Filtering (5–15 Hz)**

A cascaded **low-pass** (cutoff ~15 Hz, int gain 32) and **high-pass** (cutoff ~5 Hz) filter isolates the QRS energy.

**Stage 2: Differentiator**

$$
y[n] = \dfrac{1}{8}\bigl(2x[n] + x[n-1] - x[n-3] - 2x[n-4]\bigr)
$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**
- Step a: $2x[n]$ → current sample, weighted ×2.
- Step b: $+ x[n-1]$ → past sample.
- Step c: $- x[n-3]$ → earlier sample (subtract to emphasize slope change).
- Step d: $- 2x[n-4]$ → oldest sample, weighted ×2.
- Step e: Divide by 8 (sum of absolute coefficients) to normalize the gain.

**Stage 3: Squaring**

$$
z[n] = (y[n])^2
$$

This makes all values non-negative and **amplifies large slopes** (the sharp QRS transitions) disproportionately — exactly what we want.

**Stage 4: Moving-Window Integration**

$$
w[n] = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} z[n-k]
$$

A typical window $N = 30$ samples (at 200 Hz ≈ 150 ms) covers the QRS width.

**Stage 5: Adaptive Thresholding**

$$
\boxed{THR_{i+1} = 0.7 \cdot THR_i + 0.3 \cdot PEAK_i}
$$

This recursive update ensures the threshold **tracks slow changes** in signal amplitude (e.g., patient movement, electrode drift).

**Final Output:** Locations where $w[n]$ exceeds $THR$ AND a local maximum is detected → **R-peak locations**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Diagnostic Fingerprint)

| Arrhythmia | HR (bpm) | Rhythm | P wave | QRS width | R–R pattern |
|------------|----------|--------|--------|-----------|-------------|
| Normal Sinus | 60–100 | Regular | Normal, precedes each QRS | Narrow (≤ 0.10 s) | Constant |
| Sinus Tachycardia | > 100 | Regular | Normal | Narrow | Constant, short |
| Sinus Bradycardia | < 60 | Regular | Normal | Narrow | Constant, long |
| Atrial Fibrillation | 100–180 | **Irregularly irregular** | Absent (fibrillatory) | Narrow | Chaotic |
| Atrial Flutter | 75–150 (2:1 block) | Regular | Sawtooth F waves | Narrow | Constant |
| PAC | Normal | Slightly irregular | Abnormal (early) | Narrow | Short then long |
| PVC | Normal | Irregular | None before PVC | **Wide (> 0.12 s)** | Short then compensatory pause |
| VT | 100–250 | Regular | None | **Wide** | Constant, fast |
| VF | None (agonal) | Chaotic | None | Not discernible | Random |
| 1° AV Block | Normal | Regular | Normal | Narrow | **PR > 0.20 s** |
| 2° AV Block (Mobitz I) | Normal | Irregular (grouped) | Normal | Narrow | Progressive PR → drop |
| 2° AV Block (Mobitz II) | Normal | Irregular | Normal | Narrow | Sudden QRS drop |
| 3° AV Block | 20–50 (junctional) | Regular but **AV-dissociated** | Normal P at own rate | Narrow or wide | P and QRS march separately |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Arrhythmia Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ECG Electrodes on Patient] --> B[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 1000]
    B --> C[Analog Band-pass Filter 0.5 to 40 Hz]
    C --> D[50 Hz Notch Filter Power-line Rejection]
    D --> E[ADC Sampling at 500 Hz]
    E --> F[Digital Baseline Wander Removal]
    F --> G[Pan-Tompkins R-Peak Detection]
    G --> H[R-R Interval Computation]
    H --> I[Feature Extraction HR SDNN RMSSD QRS width]
    I --> J{Classifier Threshold or ML Model}
    J --> K[Normal Sinus Rhythm]
    J --> L[PVC Detection]
    J --> M[Atrial Fibrillation]
    J --> N[Heart Block]
    J --> O[Alert or Display on Monitor]
```

---

## 4.2 Schematic — Cardiac Conduction System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SA[SA Node Pacemaker 60-100 bpm] -->|Internodal Pathways| AV[AV Node Delay 0.1 s]
    AV -->|Bundle of His| BB[Bundle Branches Left and Right]
    BB -->|Purkinje Fibers| V[Ventricular Myocardium]
    V -->|Mechanical Contraction| P[Cardiac Output]
    P -.Feedback.-> SA
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — R-Peak Detection Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SampleAcquired
    SampleAcquired --> Filtered: Bandpass 5-15 Hz
    Filtered --> Differentiated: Apply 5-point derivative
    Differentiated --> Squared: y equals y squared
    Squared --> Integrated: Moving window sum
    Integrated --> ThresholdCheck: Compare with adaptive THR
    ThresholdCheck --> PeakDetected: Exceeds THR
    ThresholdCheck --> SampleAcquired: Below THR
    PeakDetected --> RPeakStored: Update buffer
    RPeakStored --> RRCompute: Calculate R-R interval
    RRCompute --> ThresholdCheck
    RRCompute --> Classify: Apply classifier
    Classify --> [*]
```

---

## 4.4 Comparison Chart — Normal vs Arrhythmic Waveforms (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Normal
        N1[P wave] --> N2[PR segment] --> N3[QRS complex] --> N4[ST segment] --> N5[T wave]
    end
    subgraph AF
        A1[Chaotic fibrillatory baseline] --> A2[QRS irregular]
    end
    subgraph PVC
        V1[Sinus beat] --> V2[Wide bizarre QRS no P] --> V3[Compensatory pause] --> V4[Sinus beat]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define arrhythmia. List any four major classes of cardiac arrhythmias with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer:**

**Arrhythmia** is any deviation from the normal sinus rhythm of the heart, where the SA node fails to maintain a regular rate of 60–100 bpm.

Four major classes:
1. **Supraventricular arrhythmias** – Origin above ventricles; e.g., Atrial Fibrillation.
2. **Ventricular arrhythmias** – Origin in ventricles; e.g., Ventricular Tachycardia.
3. **Conduction blocks** – Disrupted AV conduction; e.g., Third-degree AV block.
4. **Sinus node disorders** – SA node dysfunction; e.g., Sinus bradycardia.

*(Examiner expects: definition + 4 classes, each with an example, in ≤ 80 words.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the significance of the R–R interval in ECG analysis? How is heart rate computed from it?**

**Model Answer:**

The **R–R interval** is the time between two successive R-peaks, reflecting the duration of one complete cardiac cycle.

**Significance:**
- Determines instantaneous **heart rate**.
- Foundation of **HRV (Heart Rate Variability)** analysis.
- Reveals rhythm regularity — irregular R–R indicates arrhythmia.

**Computation:**

$$
HR = \dfrac{60}{T_{R-R}} \;\; \text{bpm}
$$

where $T_{R-R}$ is in seconds. *(3 marks = 1 for significance + 1 for formula + 1 for any one application.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In arrhythmia analysis questions, students often *lose marks* by (a) writing only definitions without numerical examples, (b) confusing the QRS width threshold (0.10 s vs 0.12 s), and (c) forgetting the units of HRV metrics (ms vs s). **Always quote units.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the Pan-Tompkins algorithm for R-peak detection. State each stage with its purpose.

**Model Solution:**

The **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** (Pan & Tompkins, 1985) is a real-time QRS detection algorithm. It has 6 stages:

| Stage | Operation | Purpose |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Band-pass filter (5–15 Hz)** | Isolate QRS energy, suppress P/T waves and baseline drift |
| 2 | **Differentiator** (5-point filter) | Emphasize the **high slope** of QRS complex |
| 3 | **Squaring** | Make all values non-negative, amplify large slopes |
| 4 | **Moving-window integrator** (≈ 150 ms) | Produce a smooth pulse around QRS |
| 5 | **Adaptive thresholding** | Auto-adjust detection level to amplitude drift |
| 6 | **Search-back & decision logic** | Detect missed beats (esp. in VT) |

**Key formula for differentiation:**

$$
y[n] = \tfrac{1}{8}\bigl(2x[n] + x[n-1] - x[n-3] - 2x[n-4]\bigr)
$$

**Adaptive threshold update:**

$$
THR_{i+1} = 0.7 \cdot THR_i + 0.3 \cdot PEAK_i
$$

The algorithm achieves **> 99% R-peak detection accuracy** on the MIT-BIH database. *(7 marks = 1 each stage + 1 formula + 1 result/justification.)*

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] An ECG was recorded at $f_s = 500$ Hz. The first 6 R-peaks occur at samples $\{248, 678, 1110, 1545, 1975, 2408\}$. Compute: (i) R–R intervals, (ii) Mean HR, (iii) SDNN, (iv) Classify the rhythm.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) R–R intervals (in seconds):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{R-R,1} &= \dfrac{678 - 248}{500} = \dfrac{430}{500} = 0.860 \text{ s} \\
T_{R-R,2} &= \dfrac{1110 - 678}{500} = \dfrac{432}{500} = 0.864 \text{ s} \\
T_{R-R,3} &= \dfrac{1545 - 1110}{500} = \dfrac{435}{500} = 0.870 \text{ s} \\
T_{R-R,4} &= \dfrac{1975 - 1545}{500} = \dfrac{430}{500} = 0.860 \text{ s} \\
T_{R-R,5} &= \dfrac{2408 - 1975}{500} = \dfrac{433}{500} = 0.866 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Mean Heart Rate:**

$$
\overline{T_{R-R}} = \dfrac{0.860 + 0.864 + 0.870 + 0.860 + 0.866}{5} = \dfrac{4.320}{5} = 0.864 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\overline{HR} = \dfrac{60}{0.864} = 69.44 \text{ bpm} \approx 69 \text{ bpm}
$$

**(iii) SDNN:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Deviations: } & -0.004,\; 0.000,\; +0.006,\; -0.004,\; +0.002 \text{ s} \\
\text{Squared: } & 1.6\times 10^{-5},\; 0,\; 3.6\times 10^{-5},\; 1.6\times 10^{-5},\; 0.4\times 10^{-5} \\
\text{Sum } &= 7.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}^2 \\
\text{Variance } &= \dfrac{7.2 \times 10^{-5}}{5-1} = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}^2 \\
SDNN &= \sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.24 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4.24 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Classification:**
- HR ≈ 69 bpm → **within normal range (60–100)** ✅
- R–R intervals are nearly constant (variation < 1%) → **regular rhythm** ✅
- P wave visible, narrow QRS (assumed) → **Normal Sinus Rhythm (NSR)** ✅

**Result: Normal Sinus Rhythm.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compare the ECG features of (i) Normal Sinus Rhythm, (ii) Atrial Fibrillation, and (iii) Ventricular Tachycardia. Tabulate your answer.

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Normal Sinus Rhythm | Atrial Fibrillation (AF) | Ventricular Tachycardia (VT) |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Heart rate** | 60 – 100 bpm | 100 – 180 bpm (ventricular) | 100 – 250 bpm |
| **Rhythm** | Regular | **Irregularly irregular** | Usually regular |
| **P wave** | Normal, before every QRS | **Absent** (fibrillatory waves) | Absent |
| **PR interval** | 0.12 – 0.20 s, constant | Not measurable | Not measurable |
| **QRS width** | Narrow (≤ 0.10 s) | Narrow (≤ 0.10 s) | **Wide (> 0.12 s)** |
| **Origin** | SA node | Multiple ectopic atrial foci | Ectopic ventricular focus |
| **Clinical risk** | None | Stroke, heart failure | Can degenerate into VF → sudden cardiac death |
| **Treatment** | None | Anticoagulants, cardioversion | Anti-arrhythmics, ICD, defibrillation |

*(7 marks = 1 for table structure + 5 rows × 1 each + 1 for clinical risk/management line.)*

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Design a digital signal conditioning chain for an ECG signal contaminated with 50 Hz power-line interference, baseline wander, and muscle noise. Justify the choice of each filter.

**Model Solution:**

**Complete signal chain (in order):**

1. **Instrumentation Amplifier (Front End)**
   - Gain: 1000 (since ECG is ~1 mV, need ~1 V for ADC)
   - High CMRR (> 100 dB) to reject common-mode 50 Hz.

2. **Analog High-Pass Filter (cutoff = 0.5 Hz)**
   - **Removes baseline wander** caused by respiration (~0.25–0.5 Hz) and electrode motion.
   - Justification: Below 0.5 Hz is not cardiac in origin.

3. **Analog Low-Pass Filter (cutoff = 40 Hz)**
   - **Removes muscle noise (EMG)** and high-frequency interference.
   - Justification: QRS has most energy ≤ 40 Hz; P and T waves ≤ 10 Hz.

4. **50 Hz Notch Filter (Q ≈ 30, bandwidth ±2 Hz)**
   - **Removes power-line interference** with minimal distortion of ECG spectral content.
   - Justification: Power spectral density of ECG near 50 Hz is negligible.

5. **ADC (Sampling rate ≥ 500 Hz, resolution ≥ 12 bits)**
   - Nyquist criterion: $f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max}$ with $f_{max} = 150$ Hz (for high-fidelity QRS).
   - 12-bit resolution → 1.22 µV per LSB (with 5 V range) — adequate for clinical use.

6. **Digital Baseline Correction (Optional)**
   - High-pass IIR filter (cutoff 0.5 Hz) or wavelet-based detrending.

7. **Pan-Tompkins QRS Detector**
   - As discussed in Q-A(a).

**Justification summary (key):**

- **Order matters**: Removing 50 Hz *before* baseline wander prevents notch-induced ringing on the wander.
- **Bandwidth 0.5 – 40 Hz** is the **clinical standard** (AHA recommendation).
- **Notch Q must be high** so that its impulse response does not ring over the QRS.

*(7 marks = 1 mark per stage × 6 stages + 1 mark for "why this order" justification.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — read this 30 minutes before the exam!**

- ✅ **Define arrhythmia** as any deviation from normal sinus rhythm (60–100 bpm).
- ✅ **Memorize ECG intervals** with units: PR 0.12–0.20 s, QRS 0.06–0.10 s, QT 0.36–0.44 s.
- ✅ **Know the 5 stages of Pan-Tompkins**: Bandpass → Differentiate → Square → Integrate → Adaptive threshold.
- ✅ **Master the formula** $HR = \dfrac{60}{T_{R-R}}$ and its inverse.
- ✅ **Remember 50–50 rule for AF**: heart rate is **irregular**, QRS is **narrow**, no P wave.
- ✅ **PVC signature**: Wide QRS (> 0.12 s), no P wave, compensatory pause.
- ✅ **VT = wide QRS + rate 100–250 bpm**; **VF = chaos = death** (defibrillate!).
- ✅ **Heart blocks**: 1° (PR > 0.20 s), 2° Type I (Wenckebach), 2° Type II, 3° (complete dissociation).
- ✅ **Sampling rate for ECG**: ≥ 500 Hz (Nyquist for 150 Hz QRS harmonics).
- ✅ **HRV time-domain metrics**: SDNN (overall), RMSSD (parasympathetic), pNN50 (%).
- ✅ **HRV frequency bands**: LF (0.04–0.15 Hz) = sympathetic + parasympathetic; HF (0.15–0.40 Hz) = parasympathetic.
- ✅ **Standard ECG bandwidth**: 0.5 – 40 Hz (clinical), 0.05 – 150 Hz (diagnostic).
- ✅ **Filter order in chain**: Instrumentation Amp → HPF (0.5 Hz) → LPF (40 Hz) → Notch (50 Hz) → ADC.
- ✅ **MIT-BIH database** is the universal benchmark for arrhythmia classifier evaluation.
- ✅ **Always write UNITS** in every numerical answer (ms, s, bpm, Hz) — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks/unit.
- ✅ **Adaptive threshold formula** for Pan-Tompkins: $THR_{i+1} = 0.7 \cdot THR_i + 0.3 \cdot PEAK_i$.
- ✅ **QTc (Bazett's)** = $QT / \sqrt{RR}$ — corrected for heart rate.

> **End of Notes — All the best for your KTU exam!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detection of premature ventricular contraction in ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL507 - PCBRL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Detection of premature ventricular contraction in ECG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Premature Ventricular Contraction (PVC)** — also termed *ventricular premature beat (VPB)* or *ventricular extrasystole* — is an abnormal ectopic heartbeat originating from an ectopic focus in the ventricular myocardium (the lower chambers of the heart) rather than from the sinoatrial (SA) node, the heart's natural pacemaker. On the surface Electrocardiogram (ECG), a PVC is identified by:

- A **wide, bizarre QRS complex** with duration **> 120 ms** (normal QRS is 80–100 ms).
- **Absence of a preceding P wave**.
- An often **inverted or discordant T wave** opposite in direction to the major QRS deflection.
- A **full compensatory pause** — the R–R interval surrounding the PVC equals twice the normal R–R interval (i.e., the heart "skips" the next sinus beat).
- The PVC occurs **earlier than the next expected sinus beat**, hence the term *premature*.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> Detection of PVC is classified under **arrhythmia classification** in biomedical signal processing. PVCs are the most common ventricular arrhythmia and a clinically significant precursor to more dangerous ventricular tachycardia (VT) and ventricular fibrillation (VF).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a perfectly timed orchestra playing a steady rhythm, with the **conductor (SA node)** cueing every beat at exact intervals. A PVC is like a **violinist suddenly playing a loud, off-beat note** before the conductor's cue.

- The orchestra's rhythm (sinus rhythm) is briefly disrupted.
- The violin's note (PVC) is louder, longer, and sounds "wrong" because it didn't follow the conductor.
- After the disruption, the orchestra pauses for one beat (compensatory pause) and then resumes the steady rhythm.

A physician "listens" to this orchestra using the **ECG** — a graphical recording of the heart's electrical symphony. Detecting the rogue violinist requires recognizing the **timing irregularity, abnormal note shape, and absence of the conductor's lead-in beat (P wave)**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Clinical Metrics

| Parameter | Normal Value | PVC Value |
|---|---|---|
| QRS Duration | 80–100 ms | **> 120 ms** |
| Heart Rate | 60–100 bpm | Premature (early) |
| R–R Interval Variability | < 10 % | High variability around PVC |
| P wave presence | Always present | Absent before PVC |

> [!NOTE] **Definition Recap (Board-Ready)**
> A PVC is a ventricular ectopic beat, recognized on ECG by a wide QRS complex (> 120 ms), absent P wave, compensatory pause, and abnormal T wave morphology.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ECG Signal and Why PVC Detection is Hard

The ECG is a low-frequency biosignal in the range **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz**, sampled typically at **360 Hz** (clinical standard) or higher. The QRS complex is the highest-amplitude feature (R-peak ≈ **1 mV**) and is the anchor for almost all PVC detection algorithms.

Detection challenges include:
- **Baseline wander** (low-frequency noise from respiration, electrode movement).
- **Powerline interference (50/60 Hz)**.
- **Muscle artifacts (EMG)** — high-frequency noise.
- **Morphological variability** of PVCs themselves (R-on-T, interpolated, multifocal).

## 2.2 The Pan-Tompkins Algorithm — The Foundational Approach

The **Pan-Tompkins algorithm (1985)** is the gold standard for real-time QRS detection and forms the first stage of any PVC detection pipeline. It comprises five stages:

1. **Bandpass filtering (5–15 Hz)** — isolates QRS energy, suppresses baseline wander and high-frequency noise.
2. **Differentiation** — emphasizes the steep slopes of the QRS complex.
3. **Squaring** — makes all values positive and emphasizes large differences (sharpens QRS).
4. **Moving-window integration (MWI)** — smooths the squared signal over a window of ~150 ms to obtain an "energy envelope".
5. **Adaptive thresholding** — detects R-peaks using dynamically updated thresholds.

The R-peaks located by Pan–Tompkins give us the **timing of every heartbeat**, enabling R–R interval analysis — the first clue to PVC.

## 2.3 Three Pillars of PVC Detection

PVCs are detected by combining three feature families:

### Pillar 1 — Temporal (Timing) Features
- **R–R interval analysis**: A PVC is *premature* → preceding R–R is significantly shorter than the mean normal R–R.
- **Compensatory pause check**: The R–R interval spanning the PVC is ≈ 2 × mean R–R.

### Pillar 2 — Morphological Features
- **QRS width** — measure width at half-maximum or threshold crossing.
- **QRS amplitude and shape** — compare with running template of normal beats.
- **T wave polarity** — discordant in PVC.

### Pillar 3 — Energy / Frequency Features
- **QRS energy** in the 5–15 Hz band (Pan–Tompkins output).
- **Higher-frequency content** due to abnormal ventricular conduction.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{HR}$ | $\dfrac{60}{\overline{RR}}$ | Instantaneous heart rate | bpm |
| $\overline{RR}$ | $\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} RR_i$ | Mean R–R interval | seconds |
| $RR_i$ | $t_{R_i+1} - t_{R_i}$ | Instantaneous R–R interval | seconds |
| $QRS_w$ | $t_{QRS_{end}} - t_{QRS_{start}}$ | QRS width | ms |
| $CC$ | $RR_{pre} + RR_{post}$ | Compensatory pause check | seconds |
| $T_{high}$ | $\alpha \cdot \text{SPK}$ | Adaptive high threshold | mV |
| $T_{low}$ | $\alpha \cdot \text{SPK}$ | Adaptive low threshold (typically 0.5 × high) | mV |
| $y[n]$ | $x[n]^2$ | Squaring operation (Pan-Tompkins) | mV² |
| $MWI[n]$ | $\dfrac{1}{N_w}\sum_{k=0}^{N_w-1} y[n-k]$ | Moving-window integration | mV² |
| $N_w$ | $\approx 0.150 \times F_s$ | MWI window in samples (≈ 150 ms) | samples |
| $BF$ | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Beat-detection sensitivity | — |
| $PPV$ | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Positive predictive value | — |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Boundary Conditions for PVC**
> - $QRS_w > 120$ ms
> - $RR_{pre} < 0.85 \times \overline{RR}$ (premature criterion)
> - $CC \approx 2 \times \overline{RR} \pm 10\%$ (compensatory pause)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Holter monitors and ambulatory ECG devices** — automated 24-hour PVC counting is a routine clinical requirement.
- **ICDs (Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillators)** — must distinguish PVC from noise to avoid inappropriate shocks.
- **Wearable fitness devices and smartwatches** — use simplified Pan–Tompkins variants for beat detection.
- **ICU/CCU patient monitoring systems** — real-time arrhythmia alarms rely on accurate PVC detection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Pan-Tompkins R-Peak Detection — Step-by-Step

Given a raw ECG signal $x[n]$ sampled at $F_s$ (typical $F_s = 360$ Hz):

### Step 1 — Bandpass Filtering (5–15 Hz)

$$
H_{BP}(z) = H_{LP}(z) \cdot (1 - z^{-6})
$$

where the low-pass filter $H_{LP}(z)$ is a 2nd-order Butterworth IIR with cutoff ~ 12 Hz, and the term $(1 - z^{-6})$ introduces a high-pass zeroing effect (intensity function). The cascade emphasizes QRS slopes.

**Why this works:** QRS energy is concentrated between 5–15 Hz, while P and T waves lie below 5 Hz, and EMG/muscle noise lies above 20 Hz.

### Step 2 — Differentiation

$$
d[n] = \dfrac{1}{8}\bigl(2x[n] + x[n-1] - x[n-3] - 2x[n-4]\bigr)
$$

The 5-point derivative approximates $\dfrac{dx}{dt}$ and highlights the steep upstroke and downstroke of the R-wave.

### Step 3 — Squaring

$$
y[n] = (d[n])^2
$$

All values become positive, large slopes are emphasized nonlinearly (a QRS with slope $S$ contributes $S^2$, while noise slope $s$ contributes $s^2 \ll S^2$).

### Step 4 — Moving-Window Integration

$$
MWI[n] = \dfrac{1}{N_w}\sum_{k=0}^{N_w-1} y[n-k]
$$

where $N_w = 0.150 \times F_s$. For $F_s = 360$ Hz, $N_w = 54$ samples. The MWI produces a smooth envelope that rises sharply on QRS onset and falls at offset.

### Step 5 — Adaptive Thresholding

Two thresholds track the running peak:

$$
T_{high}[n] = 0.5 \cdot \text{SPK} + 0.5 \cdot \text{NPK}
$$

$$
T_{low}[n] = 0.25 \cdot \text{SPK} + 0.25 \cdot \text{NPK}
$$

where SPK = current signal peak level, NPK = current noise peak level. A peak in MWI is flagged as a QRS if it exceeds $T_{high}$ *or* exceeds $T_{low}$ for at least 5 consecutive samples and is confirmed by the high threshold.

**Detection logic flow:**

$$
\text{If } MWI[n] > T_{high}[n] \Rightarrow \text{R-peak detected at } n
$$
$$
\text{If } T_{low}[n] < MWI[n] < T_{high}[n] \Rightarrow \text{searchback candidate}
$$
$$
\text{If no detection for } 1.66 \text{ s (corresponding to HR = 36 bpm)} \Rightarrow \text{lower threshold and searchback}
$$

## 3.2 R–R Interval Extraction and PVC Decision Rule

Let the R-peak time-stamps be $\{t_1, t_2, t_3, \ldots, t_N\}$. Compute instantaneous R–R intervals:

$$
RR_i = t_{i+1} - t_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N-1
$$

Compute a running mean over the last 8 normal beats:

$$
\overline{RR}_i = \dfrac{1}{8}\sum_{k=i-7}^{i} RR_k \quad \text{(only over normal beats)}
$$

Decision rule for PVC at beat $i$:

$$
\text{PVC}_i = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } RR_{i-1} < 0.85 \cdot \overline{RR}_{i-1} \\
  & \text{AND } QRS_w(i) > 120 \text{ ms} \\
  & \text{AND } \lvert CC - 2\overline{RR}_{i-1} \rvert < 0.10 \cdot 2\overline{RR}_{i-1} \\
  & \text{AND no } P \text{ wave in } [t_i - 200 \text{ ms}, t_i] \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Detection Trade-offs

| Method | Sensitivity (Se) | Specificity (Sp) | Real-time? | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pan-Tompkins only | ~ 99 % R-peak | Moderate | Yes | Foundation |
| Pan-Tompkins + R–R rule | ~ 95 % PVC | ~ 90 % | Yes | **Core Experiment** |
| Template matching (correlation) | ~ 97 % | ~ 96 % | Yes | Advanced |
| Wavelet transform (multi-scale) | ~ 98 % | ~ 97 % | Near real-time | Research |
| Deep learning (CNN, LSTM) | > 99 % | > 99 % | No (offline) | Current SOTA |
| MIT-BIH benchmark (state-of-art) | > 99.5 % | > 99.5 % | — | Reference |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** ECG sampled at $F_s = 360$ Hz. A series of R-peak times: $t_1 = 0.00$ s, $t_2 = 0.85$ s, $t_3 = 1.70$ s, $t_4 = 2.55$ s, $t_5 = 3.10$ s, $t_6 = 3.95$ s. The QRS width at $t_5$ is measured as $165$ ms.

**Step 1:** Compute R–R intervals

$$
RR_1 = 0.85 - 0.00 = 0.85 \text{ s}
$$
$$
RR_2 = 1.70 - 0.85 = 0.85 \text{ s}
$$
$$
RR_3 = 2.55 - 1.70 = 0.85 \text{ s}
$$
$$
RR_4 = 3.10 - 2.55 = 0.55 \text{ s} \quad \text{(premature!)}
$$
$$
RR_5 = 3.95 - 3.10 = 0.85 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute mean of normal R–R (using beats 1, 2, 3, 5):

$$
\overline{RR} = \dfrac{0.85 + 0.85 + 0.85 + 0.85}{4} = 0.85 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3:** Check prematurity

$$
0.55 \text{ s} < 0.85 \times 0.85 = 0.7225 \text{ s} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Premature criterion satisfied}
$$

**Step 4:** Check QRS width

$$
165 \text{ ms} > 120 \text{ ms} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Width criterion satisfied}
$$

**Step 5:** Check compensatory pause

$$
CC = RR_4 + RR_5 = 0.55 + 0.85 = 1.40 \text{ s}
$$
$$
2 \overline{RR} = 2 \times 0.85 = 1.70 \text{ s}
$$
$$
\lvert 1.40 - 1.70 \rvert = 0.30 \text{ s} > 0.17 \text{ s (10% threshold)} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Compensatory pause NOT full}
$$

**Conclusion:** This is a **premature ventricular contraction (PVC) at beat 5** based on the width and timing criteria, but with an *incomplete* compensatory pause (still classified as PVC, but clinically noted as interpolated or non-fully-compensated).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of PVC Detection

The complete PVC detection pipeline, from raw ECG to classified beat:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw ECG Signal x of n] --> B[Bandpass Filter 5 to 15 Hz]
    B --> C[Differentiator d of n]
    C --> D[Squaring y of n equals d n squared]
    D --> E[Moving Window Integration MWI of n]
    E --> F[Adaptive Thresholding]
    F --> G{R peak Detected}
    G --> H[Compute RR intervals]
    H --> I[Update Running Mean RR]
    I --> J[QRS Width Measurement]
    J --> K{Premature Check RR pre less than 0.85 x mean}
    K -->|Yes| L[QRS Width greater than 120 ms]
    L -->|Yes| M[Compensatory Pause Check]
    M -->|Yes| N[CLASSIFY AS PVC]
    K -->|No| O[Normal Beat]
    L -->|No| O
    M -->|No| O
    G -->|No peak| P[Searchback Lower Threshold]
    P --> G
```

## 4.2 Detailed Pan-Tompkins Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input ECG x of n] --> B[Bandpass Filter 5 15 Hz]
    B --> C[Differentiator]
    C --> D[Squaring Block]
    D --> E[MWI Window 150 ms]
    E --> F[Adaptive Thresholds]
    F --> G{Decision Logic}
    G --> H[Threshold 1 exceeded]
    G --> I[Threshold 2 searchback]
    H --> J[R peak Locked]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Output R peak Times t sub i]
```

## 4.3 PVC Decision Logic — State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Acquire_Beat
    Acquire_Beat --> Measure_QRS_Width
    Measure_QRS_Width --> WidthCheck
    WidthCheck --> WideQRS: greater than 120 ms
    WidthCheck --> Normal_Beat: less than 120 ms
    WideQRS --> CheckPWave
    CheckPWave --> NoPWave: P wave absent
    CheckPWave --> Normal_Beat: P wave present
    NoPWave --> CheckRRInterval
    CheckRRInterval --> Premature: RR pre less than 0.85 x mean
    CheckRRInterval --> Normal_Beat: RR pre normal
    Premature --> CheckCompensatoryPause
    CheckCompensatoryPause --> FullPause: CC approximately 2 x mean RR
    CheckCompensatoryPause --> PartialPause: CC deviates
    FullPause --> PVC_Confirmed
    PartialPause --> PVC_Confirmed
    PVC_Confirmed --> [*]
    Normal_Beat --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Feature Space Mapping (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Normal Beat Cluster] -->|Wide separation| B[PVC Cluster]
    A --> C[Feature 1 RR interval timing]
    A --> D[Feature 2 QRS width]
    A --> E[Feature 3 Morphology correlation]
    B --> C
    B --> D
    B --> E
    C --> F[Decision Boundary]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Classifier Linear SVM or Threshold]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Premature Ventricular Contraction (PVC). List any four characteristic ECG features used to identify a PVC.**

**Model Answer:**
A Premature Ventricular Contraction (PVC) is an ectopic cardiac beat originating from an ectopic focus in the ventricular myocardium, occurring earlier than the next expected sinus beat. Four characteristic ECG features are:

1. **Wide QRS complex** with duration > 120 ms (typically 120–200 ms).
2. **Absence of a preceding P wave**.
3. **Bizarre QRS morphology** — tall, slurred, often with notching.
4. **Discordant T wave** — T wave deflection opposite to the major QRS vector.
5. (Bonus) **Compensatory pause** following the PVC. **(Any 4)**

**[Marks Distribution: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for 4 features]**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Pan-Tompkins algorithm? Mention its major signal processing stages.**

**Model Answer:**
The Pan-Tompkins algorithm is a real-time QRS detection algorithm for ECG signals, providing high sensitivity and specificity for R-peak localization. Its five major stages are:

1. **Bandpass filtering (5–15 Hz)** — to isolate QRS energy.
2. **Differentiation** — to emphasize QRS slopes.
3. **Squaring** — to make all values positive and amplify large slopes.
4. **Moving-window integration (MWI, ~150 ms window)** — to obtain a smooth QRS energy envelope.
5. **Adaptive thresholding** — with two dynamically updated thresholds to detect R-peaks.

**[Marks Distribution: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for 5 stages]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — Choice A

**(a)** Explain in detail the morphological and temporal features used to detect a Premature Ventricular Contraction (PVC) in an ECG signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the Pan-Tompkins algorithm for QRS detection with a block diagram and explain each stage mathematically. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — Morphological & Temporal Features of PVC (7 marks)

A PVC is detected by jointly analyzing three feature families:

**1. Morphological Features (3 marks):**
- **QRS width**: Normal QRS is 80–100 ms. PVC has QRS > 120 ms due to slow ventricular cell-to-cell conduction (vs. fast His-Purkinje conduction in normal beats).
- **QRS shape**: Tall, slurred, notched, often with QS, rS, or R pattern different from the patient's normal QRS.
- **T wave**: Discordant — T wave points opposite to the main QRS deflection.
- **Absence of P wave**: No preceding atrial depolarization.
- **ST segment**: Often displaced in the direction opposite to the QRS.

**2. Temporal Features (2 marks):**
- **Prematurity**: The R–R interval preceding the PVC is shorter than the running mean R–R. A typical threshold is $RR_{pre} < 0.85 \cdot \overline{RR}$.
- **Compensatory pause**: The sum of pre- and post-PVC R–R intervals ≈ $2\overline{RR}$ (full compensatory pause).

**3. Energy Features (2 marks):**
- **QRS energy in 5–15 Hz band** (from Pan-Tompkins MWI output) is high.
- **Higher-frequency content** of the PVC QRS often exceeds that of normal beats due to abnormal conduction paths.

These features together form the input vector to a classifier (threshold-based, linear discriminant, SVM, or neural network).

#### Model Solution to (b) — Pan-Tompkins Algorithm (7 marks)

**Block diagram (1 mark):**

```
ECG → Bandpass → Differentiator → Squaring → MWI → Thresholding → R-peaks
```

**Stage 1 — Bandpass Filter (5–15 Hz) (1 mark):**
Realized as a cascade of low-pass (2nd-order Butterworth, fc = 12 Hz) and high-pass (fc = 5 Hz) filters. Output $x_1[n]$ has reduced baseline wander, 50/60 Hz interference, and EMG noise.

**Stage 2 — Differentiator (1.5 marks):**
$$
d[n] = \dfrac{1}{8}\bigl(2x_1[n] + x_1[n-1] - x_1[n-3] - 2x_1[n-4]\bigr)
$$
This 5-point derivative approximates $\frac{dx}{dt}$ and amplifies the steep QRS slopes.

**Stage 3 — Squaring (1 mark):**
$$
y[n] = \bigl(d[n]\bigr)^2
$$
Makes all values positive, nonlinearly emphasizes large slopes (QRS dominant), suppresses low-amplitude features (P, T waves).

**Stage 4 — Moving-Window Integration (1.5 marks):**
$$
MWI[n] = \dfrac{1}{N_w}\sum_{k=0}^{N_w-1} y[n-k], \quad N_w = 0.150 \times F_s
$$
For $F_s = 360$ Hz, $N_w = 54$ samples. Produces a smooth envelope peaked at the QRS center.

**Stage 5 — Adaptive Thresholding (1 mark):**
Two thresholds track signal and noise peak levels:
$$
T_1 = 0.5 \cdot \text{SPK} + 0.5 \cdot \text{NPK}
$$
$$
T_2 = 0.25 \cdot \text{SPK} + 0.25 \cdot \text{NPK}
$$
An R-peak is detected when MWI exceeds $T_1$ (or $T_2$ with a 5-sample confirmation rule). SPK and NPK are updated after each detection to adapt to beat-to-beat amplitude variations.

**[Total: 7 marks]**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — Choice B (Alternative)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete pipeline for automatic detection of PVC in ECG. Describe any two feature extraction techniques used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A patient ECG recorded at $F_s = 250$ Hz shows R-peaks at $t = 0.0, 0.90, 1.78, 2.10, 2.98$ seconds. The QRS width at the 4th beat is 160 ms. Using the standard PVC criteria, determine whether the 4th beat is a PVC. Show all calculations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — PVC Detection Pipeline (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Raw ECG → Pre-processing (Filtering) → QRS Detection (Pan-Tompkins) 
       → R-R Interval Extraction → Feature Extraction 
       → Classification (Threshold / ML) → PVC / Normal Decision
```

**Pre-processing (1 mark):**
- Notch filter (50 Hz) to remove powerline interference.
- Bandpass filter (0.5–40 Hz) to remove baseline wander and high-frequency noise.

**QRS Detection using Pan-Tompkins (1 mark):**
Provides R-peak locations $\Rightarrow$ enables R–R interval computation.

**Feature Extraction — Two Techniques (2 marks):**

**Technique 1 — R–R Interval Histogram / Variability:**
Construct histogram of R–R intervals. PVCs produce outliers on the *short* side of the histogram. Compute:
- Mean R–R
- Standard deviation of R–R
- Prematurity index: $RR_{pre} / \overline{RR}$

**Technique 2 — QRS Width & Morphology:**
- QRS width measured at half-maximum amplitude or threshold-crossing.
- Template correlation: compute correlation coefficient between current beat and stored normal template. PVCs typically have $r < 0.85$.

**Classification (1 mark):**
- Threshold-based: Apply the multi-criterion rule (width > 120 ms, prematurity, compensatory pause).
- Or ML-based: SVM, Random Forest, CNN trained on MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database.

#### Model Solution to (b) — Numerical PVC Detection (7 marks)

**Given data:**
- $F_s = 250$ Hz
- R-peaks: $t_1 = 0.00$ s, $t_2 = 0.90$ s, $t_3 = 1.78$ s, $t_4 = 2.10$ s, $t_5 = 2.98$ s
- QRS width at beat 4 = 160 ms

**Step 1 — Compute R–R intervals (2 marks):**
$$
RR_1 = 0.90 - 0.00 = 0.90 \text{ s}
$$
$$
RR_2 = 1.78 - 0.90 = 0.88 \text{ s}
$$
$$
RR_3 = 2.10 - 1.78 = 0.32 \text{ s}
$$
$$
RR_4 = 2.98 - 2.10 = 0.88 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute mean R–R of normal beats (2 marks):**
Normal R–R values (excluding the suspicious $RR_3$): $RR_1 = 0.90$, $RR_2 = 0.88$, $RR_4 = 0.88$.

$$
\overline{RR}_{normal} = \dfrac{0.90 + 0.88 + 0.88}{3} = \dfrac{2.66}{3} = 0.8867 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Prematurity check (1 mark):**
$$
RR_3 = 0.32 \text{ s}, \quad 0.85 \times 0.8867 = 0.7537 \text{ s}
$$
$$
0.32 < 0.7537 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Premature criterion SATISFIED}
$$

**Step 4 — QRS width check (1 mark):**
$$
160 \text{ ms} > 120 \text{ ms} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Width criterion SATISFIED}
$$

**Step 5 — Compensatory pause check (1 mark):**
$$
CC = RR_3 + RR_4 = 0.32 + 0.88 = 1.20 \text{ s}
$$
$$
2 \times \overline{RR}_{normal} = 2 \times 0.8867 = 1.7733 \text{ s}
$$
$$
\text{Deviation} = \dfrac{\lvert 1.20 - 1.7733 \rvert}{1.7733} \times 100\% = 32.3\%
$$
$$
32.3\% > 10\% \quad \Rightarrow \text{Full compensatory pause NOT present}
$$

**Conclusion (Bonus for completeness):**
Beat 4 satisfies the **prematurity** and **QRS width** criteria, but **lacks a full compensatory pause** (deviation 32.3%). Based on the two strongest criteria (width and prematurity), beat 4 is classified as a **Premature Ventricular Contraction (PVC)**, likely an *interpolated* or *non-fully-compensated* PVC.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — How Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the QRS width criterion**: Many students only check R–R interval and miss the wide-QRS criterion, losing 2–3 marks. **Always check at least two of: width, prematurity, compensatory pause.**
> 2. **Confusing P wave and T wave in detection**: PVCs have *no P wave*. Confusing absent P wave with inverted T wave loses marks.
> 3. **Wrong mean R–R calculation**: Including the PVC's R–R in the mean *contaminates* the average. Exclude the suspicious R–R from the mean.
> 4. **No block diagram in part (a)**: KTU examiners explicitly allocate **1–2 marks** for the block diagram. A textual description alone is insufficient.
> 5. **Not stating the threshold value (120 ms)**: Always quote the explicit threshold. "Wide QRS" alone is incomplete.
> 6. **Pan-Tompkins stages**: Writing "filter, differentiate, square, integrate, threshold" without naming the *bandwidth* (5–15 Hz) and *window size* (150 ms) loses partial marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PVC Definition**: Ectopic ventricular beat — wide QRS (>120 ms), no P wave, compensatory pause.
- **Pan-Tompkins Stages** (memorize in order): **B**andpass (5–15 Hz) → **D**ifferentiate → **S**quare → **M**WI (150 ms) → **A**daptive threshold — *mnemonic: "**BDSM-A**"*.
- **Three PVC Detection Criteria**: Premature R–R (< 0.85 × mean), QRS width > 120 ms, compensatory pause (CC ≈ 2 × mean R–R).
- **Excluding the PVC's R–R** from the running mean is critical for accurate detection — *common KTU trap*.
- **Sampling frequency** of clinical ECG: **360 Hz** (MIT-BIH standard). At this rate, 1 sample = 2.78 ms; 150 ms window = 54 samples.
- **Compensatory pause tolerance**: ±10% of $2\overline{RR}$ is generally accepted as "full".
- **Real-world implementation** is in embedded C/HDL on Holter monitors and ICDs; KTU PCBRL507 experiments often use MATLAB/Python with PhysioNet databases.
- **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database** is the standard benchmark; state-of-the-art PVC detectors achieve Se > 99%, PPV > 99%.
- **Common KTU question types**: (i) "Explain Pan-Tompkins stages with block diagram" (7 marks), (ii) "Numerical: classify beats using R–R data" (7 marks), (iii) "List PVC features" (3 marks).
- **Difference from PAC (Premature Atrial Contraction)**: PAC has a *narrow* QRS and an *abnormal P wave*; PVC has a *wide* QRS and *no P wave*. This distinction is a frequent 3-mark question.
